books.title: Around the World in Eighty Days
books.author: Jules Verne

pins.name: Longbourn
pins.story: The Bennet home: an entailed estate of two thousand a year, five unmarried daughters and a father barricaded in his library. The novel opens here, closes here, and keeps coming back to catch its breath - and Mr Collins, who will inherit the place, proposes accordingly.

image.caption: Colney House, Hertfordshire - a modest Georgian country seat of the Longbourn kind, engraved in the 1820s. Indicative of the neighbourhood; no original for Longbourn exists.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, engraved by T. Barber, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1825) · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Meryton
pins.story: The market town a mile's walk away: aunt Philips, the milliner's shop, and - from October - the militia's winter quarters. Its assembly is where Darcy declines to be introduced to anybody and is settled to be the proudest, most disagreeable man in the world.

image.caption: A Hertfordshire village near Ware, drawn in 1814 - the country Meryton's little market town would have sat in. Indicative; Austen named no original.
image.credit: John Linnell, 'View of Amwell Hill, near Ware, in Hertfordshire' (1814) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Netherfield Park
pins.story: The house Bingley takes by Michaelmas, and the question that opens the novel. Jane's providential cold buys both elder sisters a five-day residence; the ball in late November knots up half the plot, and the party's abrupt remove to town unties none of it.

image.caption: Panshanger, Hertfordshire - a substantial county house in its park, of the kind Bingley takes at Netherfield, engraved in 1818. Indicative; no original for Netherfield has ever stuck.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, etched by J. Webb, engraved by J. Bishop, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1818) · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Gracechurch Street
pins.story: The Gardiners' comfortable house in the trading City - the novel's London base and its moral counterweight: the relations Darcy should blush for prove to be the ones he ends by loving. Jane winters here unhappily; the hunt for Wickham is run from here; Lydia is married from here.

image.caption: Cheapside and Bow Church, in the trading City a few streets from Gracechurch Street, engraved 1837 - the merchant London of the Gardiners.
image.credit: Engraved by W. Albutt after Thomas H. Shepherd, published 1837 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: town
pins.story: Darcy's town house - his London base between Netherfield, Rosings and Pemberley, and (though the novel keeps it quiet) where he directs the secret hunt that saves Lydia.

image.caption: George Street, Hanover Square, in 1812 - a fashionable Mayfair street of the kind where Darcy kept the town house Austen never names, and from which he quietly directed the hunt that saved Lydia.
image.credit: Ackermann's Repository of Arts, 1812 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Hunsford Parsonage, and Rosings Park
pins.story: Mr Collins's parsonage, with Rosings and its glazing across the lane. Elizabeth's six-week Easter visit collects the novel's hinge: Darcy's first proposal, delivered with more pride than love, and the letter next morning that starts the long revision of everything she thought she knew.

image.caption: Godmersham Park, Kent - a great house in its wooded park, of the Rosings kind (and, as it happens, Austen's own brother's seat), engraved in the 1820s. Indicative; neither Rosings nor Hunsford has a settled original.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, engraved by T. Jeavons, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1826) · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: The Bell, at Bromley
pins.story: Where Lady Catherine's condescension extends to route-planning: change horses at Bromley, mention her name at the Bell, and you will be attended to. Elizabeth and Maria duly pass this way out of Kent.

pins.name: Brighton
pins.story: The camp, the sea, and a whole campful of officers: everything Lydia's imagination requires. Mr Bennet lets her go as the cheaper way of teaching her her own insignificance - a calculation the third volume spends fifty pages correcting.

image.caption: The Royal Pavilion, Brighton - the Regency resort in its heyday, from Nash's Views of the Royal Pavilion, 1826.
image.credit: From John Nash, Views of the Royal Pavilion, Brighton (1826) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Ramsgate
pins.story: Backstory, not itinerary - no track touches it. The summer before the novel, Wickham followed fifteen-year-old Georgiana Darcy here and talked her into an elopement her brother forestalled by a day or two. Darcy's letter discloses it; it is why Lydia's flight lands on him like a repetition.

image.caption: Ramsgate town and harbour, the Kent watering-place, in an aquatint of 1799.
image.credit: James Ward, 'View of Ramsgate Town and Harbour' (1799) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Lambton
pins.story: Mrs Gardiner's girlhood town, and the inn where the tour pauses - not five miles from Pemberley. Darcy and Georgiana call; the return visits multiply; and it is here, with Darcy in the room, that Jane's two letters arrive and the Lydia disaster breaks.

image.caption: Dovedale, in the Derbyshire Peak - one of the 'celebrated beauties' Austen names, standing here for the wooded Peak country of Lambton. Indicative; Lambton is a conjectured place.
image.credit: Joseph Wright of Derby, 'Dovedale' (c.1786) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Pemberley
pins.story: The house that turns the novel: the stream swelled without artificial appearance, the housekeeper's testimony, 'of this place I might have been mistress' - and the owner home a day early, walking up from the stables a changed man. The second half of the book starts on this lawn.

image.caption: Matlock Bath in the Derwent valley - the Derbyshire Peak country Pemberley is set among, with the river winding below the crags, painted in 1789. Indicative; Pemberley is a conjectured place.
image.credit: William Day, 'Matlock Baths, Derbyshire' (1789) · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Chatsworth
pins.story: One of 'the celebrated beauties' the tourists take in on the road north - and the quiet joke of the Pemberley debate: Elizabeth sees Chatsworth as Chatsworth, days before Pemberley overwhelms her as Pemberley. The adaptations have been merrily conflating the two ever since.

image.caption: Chatsworth House, Derbyshire, in an engraving of 1880
image.credit: F. O. Morris, Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen, 1880 · public domain

pins.name: Newcastle
pins.story: Wickham's commission in the regulars - Darcy's money, nobody's thanks - and the map's furthest, most grudging point: the Wickhams' marriage banished north to cool, further than even Mrs Bennet can conveniently visit.

image.caption: Newcastle upon Tyne from across the river, the map's furthest, most grudging point, in a steel engraving of 1837.
image.credit: 'Newcastle-on-Tyne', steel engraving, 1837 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Gateshead Hall
pins.story: The Reeds' house, where the novel opens: Jane a despised orphan-ward, shut in the haunted red-room, and at last shipped off to charity school. She is called back here years later to a dying, unrepenting aunt, and learns of the uncle in Madeira who will one day make her rich.

image.caption: Stone Gappe, near Lothersdale in the West Riding - the genteel Georgian house that Charlotte Brontë knew as a governess and drew on for Gateshead Hall, photographed in 1899. It stands in here for the aunt's cold house, which the novel never locates.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Lowood Institution
pins.realName: Cowan Bridge, on the Lancashire–Yorkshire border
pins.story: The charity school, cold, half-starved and preached at by the hypocrite Brocklehurst. Here Jane meets the patient Helen Burns and the kind Miss Temple - and here a typhus spring kills off the weakest girls, Helen among them. Reforms follow, and Jane stays eight years, six as pupil and two as teacher.

pins.name: Brocklebridge churchyard
pins.story: Where Helen Burns is buried. For fifteen years her grave was only a grassy mound; then a grey marble tablet marked it, inscribed with her name and the single word 'Resurgam' - I shall rise again.

image.caption: A churchyard above the River Lune at Kirkby Lonsdale, close by Cowan Bridge, painted by Turner around 1818 - indicative of Brocklebridge churchyard, the burial ground of the parish the school walked to, where Helen Burns is laid to rest.
image.credit: J. M. W. Turner, 'Kirkby Lonsdale Churchyard', c.1818 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Thornfield Hall
pins.realName: North Lees Hall, near Hathersage, Derbyshire
pins.story: Mr Rochester's house, and the heart of the book. Jane comes as governess to little Adèle; she and Rochester fall in love across a winter of fireside talk; and above them, behind a locked door, his mad wife Bertha Mason sets fires and bites in the night. The wedding is stopped at the altar by the revelation of that wife - and Jane flees. In her absence Bertha burns the hall to the ground.

image.caption: The Rydings, Birstall - a battlemented stone hall Charlotte Brontë stayed at, and one of the early models proposed for Thornfield, photographed in 1899. It stands in here for the mood of the house; the settled favourite for Thornfield is North Lees Hall near Hathersage, of which no period photograph is in the public domain.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Hay
pins.story: The village two miles off, on the road where Jane, out to post a letter on a frosty afternoon, first meets Rochester - his horse slipping on the ice of the causeway, its rider swearing, neither of them yet knowing the other.

image.caption: The main street of Hathersage, Derbyshire - the real village below North Lees Hall that Brontë used for Hay (and, later, for Morton), photographed in 1899. This is the country of the lane where Jane first meets Rochester.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Millcote
pins.story: The busy manufacturing town six miles from Thornfield, where the coaches set Jane down, where Rochester's guests come from and his errands are run, and where he buys the finery and the carriage for a wedding that never happens.

image.caption: Leeds from Beeston Hill in 1816 - mill chimneys smoking beyond fields hung with drying cloth, in Turner's view of the West Riding manufacturing town. It stands in for Brontë's invented Millcote, where Jane steps down from the coach.
image.credit: J. M. W. Turner, 'Leeds', 1816 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Whitcross
pins.realName: A crossroads on the Peak moors
pins.story: Where the coach sets Jane down when her money runs out, penniless after the aborted wedding. She leaves her one parcel behind in the coach and is 'absolutely destitute' - and for two days and nights she wanders and sleeps on the moor, begging bread, before she is taken in.

image.caption: The open moor near Hathersage - gritstone boulders, heather and a flat-topped edge running to the horizon, photographed in 1899. This is the real Peak moorland beneath the fictional Whitcross, where Jane is set down penniless and wanders destitute.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Moor House, or Marsh End
pins.story: The house on the moor where St John Rivers and his sisters Diana and Mary take the dying Jane in off their doorstep. Nursed back, she finds a home at last - and in time the Rivers prove to be her own cousins, and the Madeira uncle's fortune, split four ways, makes them all comfortable.

image.caption: Moorseats, a gritstone house on the hillside above Hathersage that Brontë knew in 1845 and is traditionally taken as the model for the Rivers' Moor House, photographed in 1899. It stands in for the door at which the destitute Jane is taken in.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Morton
pins.story: The moorland parish where St John is clergyman. Jane keeps the village girls' school here, refuses St John's cold offer to make her a missionary's wife in India, and hears - across the miles, on the wind - Rochester's voice calling her name, the moment that sends her back to Thornfield.

image.caption: The spired parish church of Hathersage, Derbyshire - the real village Brontë stayed at in 1845 and used for St John's parish of Morton, photographed in 1899. This is the church of the passage where Jane hears him preach.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Ferndean Manor
pins.realName: A wooded manor deep in the Peak
pins.story: Rochester's other, hidden house, where he has withdrawn - blind and maimed from the Thornfield fire, in which he tried and failed to save Bertha. Jane finds him here, and stays; the reunion, the marriage, and the slow return of a little of his sight all happen in this dripping wood.

image.caption: The ruined mullioned hall at Wycoller, buried in trees beside its old stone bridge - the manor long taken as the model for Ferndean - photographed in 1899. It stands in for Rochester's last house, 'deep buried in a wood'.
image.credit: W. R. Bland, 1899, from The Life and Works of the Sisters Brontë · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: my flat behind Langham Place
pins.story: A returned colonial, bored to death with London, comes home to find a frightened stranger on his landing - Scudder, a freelance spy who has faked his own death. Days later Scudder is knifed on the floor, and the plot he carried is now Hannay's to run with, or hang for.

image.caption: All Souls, Langham Place - the Nash church with the needle spire that Hannay gives as the landmark for his flat just behind Portland Place.
image.credit: W. F. Starling / C. J. Hullmandel, 1826 · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the wayside station on the moor
pins.story: The lonely halt where Hannay leaves the train - no road to it from anywhere, a tarn lapping on grey granite, the moor all around. The perfect country for a hunted man, and the start of five days in the open.

pins.name: Cairnsmore of Fleet
pins.story: Hannay takes to the heather beneath the great hill, curlews and plovers crying everywhere, and for the first time since the murder feels almost free - until the hunt closes in over the open moor.

pins.name: the inn among the hills
pins.story: A moorland inn kept by a bookish young man aching for adventure. Hannay tells him enough of the truth to win a night's shelter - and the lad holds off the ring's men when they come asking after him.

image.caption: Loch Trool in the Galloway hills, photographed in 1898 - indicative of the empty upland where Hannay is hunted; the inn among the hills is Buchan's invention.
image.credit: Valentine & Sons, Dundee, 1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Sir Harry's house
pins.story: Fleeing across country, Hannay blunders into a by-election and is press-ganged into speaking for Sir Harry, the earnest Liberal candidate - who, hearing the real story afterwards, believes every word and sends him on to his godfather, Sir Walter Bullivant.

pins.name: the roadman's stretch of road
pins.realName: A hill road on the moor, Galloway
pins.story: Hannay changes clothes with a roadman and mends the highway in disguise while the ring's men drive past and their monoplane quarters the sky overhead - the chase's most famous piece of nerve.

pins.name: the house in the fir plantations
pins.story: A lonely house where the hunt runs Hannay to ground - and he knows the mild, bald host for the ring's leader: the old man with a young voice who can hood his eyes like a hawk. Locked in a store-room, he blasts his way out with the enemy's own explosives.

pins.name: Artinswell, on the Kennet
pins.story: The chalk-stream water-meadows where Hannay finally reaches Sir Walter Bullivant, fishing for trout on his Whitsun holiday. Here the dead man's notebook is decoded and the plot is proved real.

image.caption: The River Kennet near Newbury, painted by John Linnell - the quiet Berkshire river-valley Buchan renames 'Artinswell', a few miles downstream of Kintbury.
image.credit: John Linnell, 1815 · Fitzwilliam Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: town
pins.story: The London rooms where the counter-plot is laid: the ring - the 'Black Stone' - means to steal the dispositions a French officer is bringing over, and Scudder's last riddle, thirty-nine steps and a high tide, must be cracked before they slip the secret out to sea.

image.caption: The Admiralty boardroom in Whitehall, photographed in 1895 - the panelled room, with its wind-dial over the fireplace, where Hannay carries his warning to the men who run the Navy.
image.credit: From Vesey Hamilton's Naval Administration, 1896 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bradgate
pins.story: The Kent seaside resort Buchan calls Bradgate, where Hannay comes down to the coast to hunt for the staircase in Scudder's note - a smooth June sea, a lightship on the sands, and a destroyer lying off the shore.

image.caption: Broadstairs and its bay about 1890 - the Kent resort Buchan renames 'Bradgate', where, laid up by illness, he actually wrote the book.
image.credit: Photochrom, Detroit Publishing Co. · Library of Congress · c.1890 · public domain

pins.name: the Ruff
pins.story: The headland where villa staircases run down to private beaches. On one of them Hannay counts thirty-nine steps, finds three ordinary Englishmen playing at innocence - the ring itself - and springs the trap, seven weeks before the war.

image.caption: The North Foreland lighthouse above the Kent chalk, near Broadstairs - the headland Buchan calls 'the Ruff', where the thirty-nine steps run down to the sea.
image.credit: James Malton, c.1791 · Yale Center for British Art (CC0) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: under the Blue Ridge
pins.story: Where Jim is orphaned at ten and sent west to grandparents he has never met. The mountains are the horizon he loses: on the prairie he finds himself, for the first time, under a sky with no familiar ridge against it.

pins.name: Bohemia
pins.story: The Shimerdas' home, and the far anchor of the transatlantic thread. A respected family here - the father a weaver and fiddler - they sell up and cross the world to the prairie. The homesickness for this place is what will kill Mr Shimerda.

image.caption: The Charles Bridge, Prague, photochrom c.1890 - the one fixed point of the Shimerdas' lost country
image.credit: Photoglob / Detroit Publishing Co., c.1890 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the port
pins.story: The North Sea port where the Shimerdas, and thousands like them, took ship for America. The last of the old world before the long crossing - and, for Mr Shimerda, the last of everything he understood.

image.caption: Steerage passengers on the deck of a Bremen emigrant liner, c.1907 - the crossing the Shimerdas made
image.credit: Bain News Service, c.1907 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: my grandfather's farm
pins.story: Jim's grandparents' place, the only wooden house west of Black Hawk, and the base of his boyhood. The prairie-dog town, the great snake, the first blizzard, the whole raw new world open around it.

image.caption: A Nebraska homestead on the Divide - the Rawding family before their sod house, a cow grazing on the roof, Custer County, 1886
image.credit: Solomon D. Butcher, 1886 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Shimerdas' cave
pins.story: The immigrant family's first home, an earth cave dug into a bank - the wooden house and the mud cave, a mile apart, the whole gulf between the two families in miniature. Here the Shimerdas half-starve through the first winter, and here Mr Shimerda takes his own life.

pins.name: Black Hawk
pins.story: The little railroad town both families move toward: Jim's schooling, and Ántonia's years as a hired girl in other people's kitchens. the Italian showmen the Vannis and their dancing tent, the Harlings, the predatory moneylender Wick Cutter - the town where the two lives are briefly in the same place and visibly drawing apart.

pins.name: Lincoln
pins.story: The University of Nebraska, and Jim's first real step off the prairie. His tutor Gaston Cleric opens the world of books to him; and here Lena Lingard turns up too, running her own dress shop - the girl who left the Divide and thrived, briefly crossing the boy who is leaving it for good.

pins.name: Harvard
pins.story: Jim goes east to finish his studies at Harvard, following his tutor Cleric to Boston. The prairie recedes to memory. It is a stage in a rise rather than a place with scenes - the line thinning as it climbs.

pins.name: New York
pins.story: The far end of the line that left. Jim is legal counsel to a great Western railway - rich, restless, ardent about big Western dreams and becalmed in a cold marriage he no longer pretends to love. His worldly summit, and his lowest ebb. It was also, long before, the door through which the Shimerdas entered the country.

image.caption: Mulberry Street, New York, c.1900 - the teeming immigrant city the Shimerdas pass through, and Jim's cold summit years later
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Denver
pins.story: Where Ántonia's ruin happens, off-stage. She travels here to marry the railway conductor Larry Donovan; he strings her along, spends her savings, never marries her, and deserts her. She comes home to the Divide alone and pregnant. West-flung, it mirrors Jim's eastern pins: everyone is flung out from the prairie, and only some come back.

image.caption: Denver, Colorado, with the Rockies beyond, 1898 - the city where Ántonia's ruin is waiting
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., 1898 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: San Francisco
pins.story: Where Lena Lingard ends up - the leading dressmaker of the city, comfortable, independent, never married, never quite touched by any of it. The mirror of Ántonia: the one who left the prairie and prospered, against the one who stayed and rooted.

image.caption: Market Street, San Francisco, 1900 - a few years before the earthquake; the far coast where Lena thrives
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., 1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Ántonia's farm
pins.story: Ántonia's own place, twenty years on: married to the kindly Anton Cuzak, an orchard she planted herself, a fruit-cave in the bank, and ten or eleven children spilling up into the light. The earth cave that was misery in the first winter is now a larder bursting with life - the whole 'rooting into the land' made visible, and where Jim's long return finally lands.

image.caption: A prosperous two-storey sod house, Custer County, Nebraska, 1886 - the kind of settled farm Ántonia's ruin becomes
image.credit: Solomon D. Butcher, 1886 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: A village of La Mancha
pins.story: The knight's starting and ending point. He rides out from here on each of his three sallies, and is carried back here each time - the last time to regain his wits and die.

pins.name: The castle
pins.realName: A roadside inn (venta) on the Camino Real
pins.story: On the first sally, alone, he mistakes a common inn for a castle and its serving-girls for noble ladies; the amused innkeeper 'dubs' him a knight in a mock ceremony over the horse-trough.

pins.name: The giants
pins.realName: The windmills of Campo de Criptana
pins.story: The defining delusion. He charges the windmills as monstrous giants, and a turning sail catches his lance and flings him and Rocinante across the plain - while Sancho, beside him, sees only windmills.

pins.name: Puerto Lápice
pins.story: The road and inn where the delusions come thick: two flocks of sheep he takes for warring armies, a barber's brass basin he wins and wears as Mambrino's golden helmet, and an inn he insists to the last is a castle.

pins.name: The monster in the dark
pins.story: The purest form of the whole joke, after the windmills. In pitch dark a terrible rhythmic pounding stops the pair cold; Quixote arms for a monstrous battle and the terrified Sancho hobbles Rocinante so he cannot charge. At dawn the monster is revealed - six wooden fulling-hammers, thumping cloth in a mill.

pins.name: The Sierra Morena
pins.story: The wild mountains where Quixote strips to his shirt and does mad love-penance in imitation of Amadís of Gaul - and sends Sancho off with a letter to Dulcinea, the first time master and squire part.

image.caption: The Despeñaperros pass through the Sierra Morena - Carlos de Haes, c.1862
image.credit: Carlos de Haes, c.1862 · Museo del Prado / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: El Toboso
pins.story: The home of Quixote's invented lady. In Part II they hunt for her palace by night and find only a peasant girl on a donkey - whom Sancho, this time, passes off as the enchanted Dulcinea. Here the squire manufactures the delusion.

image.caption: A street in El Toboso, drawn on the spot by Daniel Vierge, 1901
image.credit: Daniel Vierge, 1901 · from Au pays de Don Quichotte (BnF / Gallica) / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: The Cave of Montesinos
pins.realName: The Cueva de Montesinos, near Ossa de Montiel
pins.story: Lowered on a long rope into a real cave, he comes up claiming an enchanted underground vision of Montesinos, Durandarte and a spellbound Dulcinea. The one delusion the book itself will not resolve - dream, invention, or true - and the one with no witness to correct him.

image.caption: The entrance to the Cueva de Montesinos, drawn on the spot by Daniel Vierge, 1901
image.credit: Daniel Vierge, 1901 · from Au pays de Don Quichotte (BnF / Gallica) / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: The enchanted bark
pins.story: Finding an unmoored fishing boat on the Ebro, Quixote declares it an enchanted bark sent to carry him to a captive knight, and takes the working water-mills midstream for a fortress. They nearly drown; Sancho points out the boat belongs to the fishermen.

image.caption: The river Ebro at Zaragoza, a boat at the bank below the basilica of El Pilar - photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photoglob Co., c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: The Duke's castle
pins.realName: The Duke and Duchess's palace (near Pedrola, Aragon)
pins.story: The Aragonese palace where two bored aristocrats, who have read Part I, stage elaborate jokes on the pair: the wooden flying-horse Clavileño, the 'disenchantment' of Dulcinea, the feigned love of Altisidora - and the promise of an island for Sancho to govern.

pins.name: The island of Barataria
pins.story: The fake 'island' the Duke gives Sancho to govern as a prank. Sancho rules it with such shrewd, plain justice that he shames the joke, then wisely abdicates and rides back to his master.

pins.name: Barcelona
pins.story: On the shore, Quixote is unhorsed by the 'Knight of the White Moon' - his neighbour Sansón Carrasco in disguise - and bound by oath to lay down his arms and go home for a year. The first place the map has nothing left to correct.

image.caption: Barcelona's harbour front at the Plaça de la Pau, the Columbus monument and the customs house - photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photoglob Co., c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the sea-reach of the Thames
pins.realName: The Sea Reach of the Thames, off Gravesend
pins.story: Where the whole tale is told. Five old friends aboard a yawl wait for the tide to turn on the Thames estuary, and Marlow - a seaman still, sitting cross-legged like an idol - begins the story of the one voyage that marked him. 'And this also,' he says, looking at London's glow, 'has been one of the dark places of the earth.'

image.caption: The Nore Light at the mouth of the Thames, painted by W. L. Wyllie c.1900 - the estuary sea-reach where the Nellie swings at her anchor and Marlow begins his tale.
image.credit: W. L. Wyllie, The Nore Light (c.1900) · Royal Museums Greenwich · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: London
pins.story: Home from the sea and out of a ship, Marlow loafs about London until his aunt's Continental connections land him the Company's appointment - skipper of a river steamboat - and a summons to Brussels to sign the contract.

image.caption: The Thames Embankment, London, photographed c.1890 - where Marlow, home from the sea, loafs about until the Company's appointment finds him.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the sepulchral city
pins.story: The Company's city, where Marlow signs the contract. In the waiting-room two women knit black wool, guarding the door; a doctor measures his skull 'in the interests of science' and asks, mildly, whether there has been madness in his family. Everything here forebodes.

image.caption: The Palais de Justice, Brussels - the monumental, tomb-still city Marlow thinks of as a whited sepulchre, where he signs on at the Company's headquarters.
image.credit: Postcard, late 19th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: a French steamer
pins.story: The French Atlantic port where Marlow takes ship for Africa - down to the coast to join the steamer that will carry him a month south, past a coast like an enigma.

image.caption: The harbour at Bordeaux, painted by Eugène Boudin in 1874 - indicative of the French Atlantic port where Marlow takes ship for the coast; the novel names only 'a French steamer'.
image.credit: Eugène Boudin, Bordeaux, the Harbour (1874) · Walters Art Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the mouth of the big river
pins.realName: Boma, near the mouth of the Congo
pins.story: The mouth of the great river, thirty days down the coast. From here the Company's launches work up the last navigable reach to the first station - the ocean voyage over, the true descent about to begin.

pins.name: the Company's station
pins.realName: Matadi, at the head of ocean navigation
pins.story: The Outer Station: a railway being blasted into the hillside to no clear purpose, boilers rusting in the grass. And in a shade off the path, the grove where the used-up labourers of the Company have crawled away to die. The book's moral centre, met in its first pages.

pins.name: the Central Station
pins.story: The Central Station, at the head of the two-hundred-mile portage. Marlow arrives to find his steamboat at the bottom of the river, and spends weeks fishing it up and patching it while the agents intrigue for advancement - and here he first hears the name Kurtz: the best agent the Company has, alone up-river, gone quiet.

pins.name: eight miles below the station
pins.realName: The river below the Inner Station
pins.story: Where the river turns deadly. A dense white fog halts the steamer at dawn; then, in the last miles below the station, arrows come out of the silent bank, and the African helmsman is killed at Marlow's feet - the price of the approach to Kurtz.

pins.name: the Inner Station
pins.story: Kurtz's station, at the head of the navigable river: a ruined house, a broken fence, and fence-posts topped with human heads turned inward. A mad Russian in harlequin patches keeps the last of the ivory and the last of Kurtz's genius. The wilderness had found the man out early, and taken him for its own.

pins.name: the head of an island
pins.realName: The head of an island, down-river
pins.story: Where Kurtz dies. Carried down-stream on a stretcher, his life going out with the brown current, he sums up everything he has seen and done and been in a whisper - 'The horror! The horror!' - and is gone. They bury something in a muddy hole, and the steamer goes on.

pins.name: Blunderstone Rookery
pins.story: David's birthplace, and the whole first act of his childhood: born here six months after his father's death, he grows up between his gentle mother and stern Peggotty, until the Murdstones arrive to marry his mother and break the house up. He is sent away, comes home to find his mother dead, and never lives here again.

image.caption: A Suffolk village under its church tower - John Constable, 'Brightwell Church and Village', 1815
image.credit: John Constable, 1815 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Yarmouth
pins.story: The flat, sea-smelling town where Peggotty's family live in a black boat drawn up on the shore. David is happiest here as a boy; it is the home of Little Em'ly and Ham, and the harbour Mr Peggotty keeps returning to. It is also where the storm comes, at the very end.

image.caption: The beach, gardens and jetty at Great Yarmouth, photographed c.1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Salem House
pins.story: The brutal boarding school run by the ignorant, caning Mr Creakle, where David is made to wear a placard reading 'Take care of him. He bites.' Here he meets the two poles of his life: gentle, doomed Traddles and the dazzling, careless Steerforth, who takes him up as a favourite.

image.caption: Blackheath, south-east London, engraved in 1808 - open heath and a windmill, the edge-of-town kind of spot where Mr Creakle's Salem House is placed.
image.credit: Samuel Noble, 1808 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Murdstone and Grinby's
pins.story: The riverside warehouse where the ten-year-old David is set to washing and labelling bottles among rough boys, lodging with the Micawbers. It is the pit of the novel: shameful, hopeless drudgery that David never quite forgives, and the source of Dickens's own lifelong grievance.

image.caption: The working Thames at Blackfriars in 1828 - wharves and barges below St Paul's, the riverside London of Murdstone and Grinby's bottling warehouse.
image.credit: Thomas H. Shepherd, Metropolitan Improvements, 1828 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Windsor Terrace
pins.story: The Micawbers' shabby-genteel lodging, where David boards during the warehouse days. Mr Micawber waits here, magnificently, for something to turn up, dodging creditors between bursts of oratory - before debt carries him off to the King's Bench prison.

image.caption: The City Road Basin in 1828, at the head of the City Road where the Micawbers took their Windsor Terrace lodging - canal barges and warehouses.
image.credit: Thomas H. Shepherd, Metropolitan Improvements, 1828 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Canterbury
pins.story: The gentle counterweight to London - though David first comes through it wayworn and ragged on his walk to Dover. Later Betsey Trotwood sends him to school at Dr Strong's here, boarding with the lawyer Mr Wickfield and his daughter Agnes, the quiet centre of David's whole life. It is also where the writhing clerk Uriah Heep spins his web, and where the Micawbers come as his tools.

image.caption: Canterbury Cathedral, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Dover
pins.story: The end of David's desperate walk from London: footsore and ragged, he finds his aunt Betsey Trotwood in her neat cottage above the sea. She takes him in, sees off the pursuing Murdstones, and - for the first time since his mother died - he is safe.

image.caption: Dover sea front, the beach and cliffs below the Castle, photographed c.1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Highgate
pins.story: On its hilltop: first the proud Mrs Steerforth's house, where David visits his charming friend and meets Rosa Dartle, Mrs Steerforth's brooding, scarred companion; and later the cottage where David brings his child-wife Dora, and where their sweet, hopeless attempt at housekeeping plays out.

image.caption: Highgate on its hill north of London, in a view after Chatelain - the real village onto which David sets the Steerforths' house and, later, his cottage with Dora.
image.credit: From Old and New London, 1873, after Chatelain (1745) · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Doctors' Commons
pins.story: The 'lazy old nook' of church-law courts where David is articled as a proctor, buying his way into a profession. Here he falls headlong for Dora Spenlow, his employer's daughter, and here he later scrapes for a living when his aunt's fortune collapses.

image.caption: The court of Doctors' Commons, aquatint from Ackermann's 'Microcosm of London', 1808
image.credit: Thomas Rowlandson & Augustus Pugin, aquatint by J. Bluck, 1808 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Putney
pins.story: Where Dora's spinster aunts take her after her father's sudden death, and where David haunts the road to court her. It is from Putney that he finally wins her, and marries her.

image.caption: Putney across the Thames - St Mary's tower and the old timber bridge, etched in 1738 - the riverside district where Dora's aunts keep house.
image.credit: Thomas Preist, 1738 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Golden Cross
pins.story: The coaching inn where David, now seventeen, puts up on returning to London a young gentleman - and by chance meets the grown-up Steerforth again, the friendship rekindling just as it will lead them both to Yarmouth, and to ruin.

image.caption: Charing Cross and the entry to the Strand in 1842, by the Charles I statue - the junction where the Golden Cross coaching inn stood, from which David takes the Dover road.
image.credit: Thomas Shotter Boys, 1842 · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Naples
pins.story: The bright southern shore where Steerforth carries Little Em'ly after their elopement, and where - grown tired of her - he leaves her, coldly offering her in marriage to his servant. It is the far point of her ruin, and the turn from which she begins the long road home.

image.caption: The harbour at Naples, with the city and the Posillipo hill beyond, photographed c.1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Gravesend
pins.story: The Thames-side port where the emigrant ship lies waiting. Here David watches the whole broken company he loves - Mr Peggotty and the reclaimed Em'ly, the Micawbers, and Martha, the fallen girl who helped find Em'ly - go aboard to sail for Australia and a new life, and waves them out towards the sea.

image.caption: A three-masted merchantman under tow on the Thames off Gravesend - Samuel Walters, 1839
image.credit: Samuel Walters, 1839 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Port Middlebay
pins.story: The far side of the world, where the emigrants make good. Mr Peggotty and Em'ly settle to a hard, honest life; and Mr Micawber, of all men, comes into his own at last - 'Port Middlebay District Magistrate', toasted at a public dinner, the eternal debtor turned pillar of a colony.

image.caption: Emigrant clippers at the Railway Pier, Sandridge (Port Melbourne), photographed c.1880
image.credit: Charles Nettleton, c.1880 · State Library Victoria · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: a valley in Switzerland
pins.realName: a valley in the Alps, near Geneva
pins.story: Where David, crushed by Dora's death and the drowning of Steerforth and Ham, wanders in grief for months. In a still mountain valley, opening a bundle of letters from Agnes, his numbness finally breaks - and he begins, slowly, to live again, and to write.

image.caption: A valley in the Bernese Alps - Alexandre Calame, 'The Jungfrau, Switzerland', c.1853–55
image.credit: Alexandre Calame, c.1853–55 · Walters Art Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the forge
pins.realName: the Hoo marshes near Cooling, Kent
pins.story: Pip's home on the Kent marshes, the forge where the gentle blacksmith Joe Gargery works and Pip's shrewish sister keeps house. The honest place Pip will grow ashamed of, and the one he comes back to.

pins.name: the churchyard
pins.story: Where the book opens: among his family's graves the orphan Pip is seized by an escaped convict, Magwitch, who terrifies him into stealing a file and food. The chance pity that will secretly make his fortune.

pins.name: the Battery
pins.realName: the marsh battery, the Hoo peninsula
pins.story: Out on the misty marsh Pip carries the stolen file and vittles to the shivering convict. Later the soldiers retake Magwitch here, locked in a death-grip with a second escaped man - his enemy, Compeyson.

pins.name: the Hulks
pins.realName: the prison ships, the Thames estuary
pins.story: The prison ship off the marshes, 'like a wicked Noah's ark', from which Magwitch has escaped - and to which he is returned before being shipped to Australia. The origin of the convict, and secretly of Pip's fortune.

image.caption: Prison hulks moored on the Thames, painted about 1814 - the decommissioned warships that held convicts awaiting transportation.
image.credit: Oil painting, British School, c.1814 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Satis House
pins.story: The stopped-clock house where the jilted Miss Havisham raises the cold, beautiful Estella to break men's hearts. Here Pip is made to feel common, falls for Estella, and comes to believe - wrongly - that his fortune and the girl are meant for him.

image.caption: Rochester, the novel’s unnamed “town” - the Norman castle, photochrom of the 1890s. Miss Havisham’s Satis House was modelled on the Elizabethan Restoration House nearby.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress (LCCN 2002708076) · public domain

pins.name: Barnard's Inn
pins.story: Pip's first London lodging, shared with the cheerful, hopeless Herbert Pocket. Where a marsh boy sets about learning to be a gentleman - and running into debt doing it.

pins.name: Mr Jaggers's office
pins.story: The office of Jaggers, the growling criminal lawyer who administers Pip's unnamed benefactor's money and guards the secret of its source. Hard by Newgate and the reek of Smithfield - the law and its meat.

pins.name: the Temple
pins.story: Pip's riverside chambers, where one stormy night the returned convict Magwitch climbs the stairs and reveals the truth: HE is the benefactor, not Miss Havisham. The gentleman was made by the marsh after all.

pins.name: Mill Pond Bank
pins.story: The quiet Rotherhithe waterside where Magwitch is hidden, under the name Provis, while Pip and Herbert plan to row him downriver and out of England aboard a foreign steamer.

pins.name: Newgate
pins.story: The prison whose shadow falls across the whole book. Wemmick walks Pip through it; and here, taken and mortally hurt, Magwitch is tried, condemned, and dies - his fortune forfeit to the Crown, Pip's expectations gone with it.

pins.name: Richmond
pins.story: Where Estella is sent to be shown off in society, and where Pip dances attendance on her, tormented, while she coolly warns him she has no heart to give - to him or anyone.

pins.name: the Lower Hope
pins.realName: Lower Hope Reach, the Thames estuary
pins.story: Where the river escape ends. As Pip's boat waits for the Hamburg steamer, a police galley closes in; Compeyson drowns, Magwitch is seized and crushed, and the plan - and the fortune - are lost on the tide.

pins.name: New South Wales
pins.story: The penal colony Magwitch is transported to. There the branded convict prospers as a sheep-farmer, hoarding every pound to make, in secret and from the far side of the world, a gentleman of the boy who once fed him on the marsh.

pins.name: Cairo
pins.story: The East, where the ruined and humbled Pip goes to work honestly for the firm he once secretly helped Herbert into. Eleven quiet years of clerking abroad before he comes home to the marsh and the ruins of Satis House.

pins.name: Lincoln's Inn Hall; the Court of Chancery
pins.realName: Lincoln's Inn / High Court of Chancery
pins.story: The fog-bound heart of everything: the Court where the interminable suit consumes fortunes and lives. The three young wards first meet here, and Richard is slowly destroyed by it.

image.caption: Lincoln's Inn Old Hall, which housed the Court of Chancery, engraved c.1830
image.credit: Thomas H. Shepherd, c.1830 · public domain

pins.name: Cook's Court, Cursitor Street
pins.story: Where Mr Snagsby the law-stationer pursues his lawful calling, and copies of a certain law-writer's hand set the whole secret in motion.

pins.name: Krook's Rag and Bottle Warehouse
pins.realName: A court by Chancery Lane / Star Yard (fictional shop, real district)
pins.story: The rag-and-bottle shop where Nemo lodges and where Krook, its gin-soaked master, dies by spontaneous combustion - the most notorious death in Dickens.

image.caption: Old timber houses at the corner of Chancery Lane - the mouldering district of Krook's rag-and-bottle shop - wood engraving from Old and New London, 1873
image.credit: Old and New London (1873), after J. T. Smith · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the hemmed-in churchyard - the archway, the step, the iron gate
pins.realName: A pestilential pauper burial ground (best-fit: Russell Court, off Drury Lane)
pins.story: The iron gate the whole novel circles back to. Nemo is 'sown in corruption' here; Jo leads the veiled Lady Dedlock to it; and here, in the snow, she dies on the step.

image.caption: Enon Chapel - a notorious London burial pit with a dance hall above the bodies, the pestilential kind of ground Dickens was attacking
image.credit: The Poor Man's Guardian, 1847 · London Museum · public domain

pins.name: Tom-all-Alone's
pins.realName: A St Giles-type rookery (composite; St Giles-in-the-Fields the leading model)
pins.story: The black, tumbledown street where Jo lives, and where the fever breeds that will climb out of the slum and reach even the great house.

image.caption: Dudley Street, Seven Dials - the St Giles rookery behind Tom-all-Alone's, engraved by Gustave Doré, 1872
image.credit: Gustave Doré, London: A Pilgrimage, 1872 · public domain

pins.name: Mr. George's Shooting Gallery
pins.realName: Near Leicester Square / the Haymarket (fictional gallery, real quarter)
pins.story: The old trooper's gallery, a refuge on the edge of the respectable world - where Jo is hidden, and where he dies with Woodcourt beside him.

image.caption: Harmony in Leicester Square - street musicians before Jaunay's Hotel in the raffish foreign-hotel quarter of Mr George's gallery, lithograph, 1848
image.credit: Lithograph after H. Mörner, printed by Graf & Soret, 1848 · British Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: my Lady Dedlock's house in town
pins.realName: The Dedlocks' London town house (unnamed West End square)
pins.story: The cold, grand house where Lady Dedlock keeps her freezing state - and from which, one winter night, she walks out for the last time.

image.caption: St James's Square, London, in a 1753 engraving - the sort of grand West End square, ringed by aristocratic mansions, where the Dedlocks keep their cold town house, and from which my Lady walks out one winter night for the last time.
image.credit: Thomas Bowles, 'A View of St James's Square', 1753 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Greenleaf (Miss Donny's house)
pins.story: Where the lonely, unwanted Esther is quietly schooled for six years, until the letter comes that summons her to London and the story.

image.caption: The Market Place at Reading - the town where Esther is schooled - one of the earliest photographs, a calotype by Fox Talbot, c.1845
image.credit: William Henry Fox Talbot, c.1845 · Reading Borough Libraries · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bleak House
pins.realName: Bleak House, near St Albans, Hertfordshire (fictional)
pins.story: Jarndyce's rambling, kindly house - the one warm centre of the book, and Esther's home. Jo is found feverish at the brick-kilns nearby; it is the fixed point all her journeys return to.

image.caption: High Street, St Albans - the old market town Bleak House lies just beyond - hand-coloured aquatint, c.1807
image.credit: Engraved from a drawing by G. Shepherd for The Beauties of England and Wales, c.1807 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Chesney Wold
pins.realName: Chesney Wold - a Lincolnshire seat (fictional; atmosphere drawn from Rockingham Castle)
pins.story: The great, damp, ancestral house where Lady Dedlock is bored to death and the rain falls for ever on the Ghost's Walk - the cold pole of the story, opposite Bleak House's warmth.

image.caption: Rockingham Castle - the real house whose mood Dickens gave Chesney Wold, not the imagined seat itself
image.credit: J. P. Neale, 1818 · British Library · public domain

pins.name: Deal
pins.story: The windy garrison town on the Kent coast where Richard, in uniform now, is quartered - and where Esther, come to reason with him, watches Allan Woodcourt come ashore from a shipwreck.

image.caption: The promenade at Deal, Kent, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: 221B, Baker Street
pins.story: Where the case begins. Dr Mortimer lays before Holmes a walking-stick, an old manuscript of the Baskerville curse, and the fresh death of Sir Charles Baskerville on his own moor - beside the print of a gigantic hound.

pins.name: a small wayside station
pins.story: Where Watson and Sir Henry leave the train and the soft country ends. A wagonette waits to carry them up onto the moor, and armed soldiers watch the roads for an escaped convict.

image.caption: Mist lifting off the open moor - the brooding Dartmoor the train delivers them to. Edmund Morison Wimperis, 1896.
image.credit: Edmund Morison Wimperis, 1896 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Baskerville Hall
pins.story: The grim old pile at the heart of the story: a heavy hall of grey stone ringed by the brooding moor, where the last Baskerville comes home to a curse and, at night, the long low cry the moor-folk call the Hound.

image.caption: A gaunt gothic manor of the period, standing for Doyle's invented Baskerville Hall - Newlands Manor, photographed by Francis Frith, c.1900.
image.credit: Francis Frith, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the hamlet of Grimpen
pins.story: The scatter of moor cottages that serves the Hall - the doctor's house, the little post office through which a crucial telegram is traced, and the folk who know the moor and fear it.

image.caption: The moor near Princetown at dusk - the kind of country the scattered hamlet of Grimpen sits in.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1903 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Merripit House
pins.story: The naturalist Stapleton's lonely house against the mire - and the trap at the centre of the plot. From here the Hound is loosed; here Sir Henry is asked to dine and sent home alone across the dark moor.

pins.name: the great Grimpen Mire
pins.story: The deadly green bog that only Stapleton knows how to cross, its safe path marked by wands. It swallows the moor ponies whole - and, in the end, takes the murderer himself, and keeps him.

image.caption: A Dartmoor mire (Taw Marsh) - the treacherous bog country of the moor; Doyle's Grimpen Mire is Fox Tor Mire, a little to the south.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1903 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the old stone huts
pins.story: The grey Neolithic huts of the ancient moor-dwellers - and the secret residence of Sherlock Holmes, who never went back to London at all, but watched the whole affair unseen from the heart of the moor.

image.caption: The Bronze-Age hut circles at Grimspound - the real 'stone huts' of the moor that Holmes's hideout is drawn from.
image.credit: From S. Baring-Gould, 'A Book of Dartmoor', 1907 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Coombe Tracey
pins.story: The little town off the moor where Laura Lyons lives - the woman who was lured into writing to Sir Charles on the night he died, and whose story unlocks the plot.

pins.name: the convict prison of Princetown
pins.story: The great prison on the moor. From it the murderer Selden has escaped, and is hiding among the tors - a second hunted thing on the moor, and the cause of a death mistaken for another.

image.caption: The gate of Dartmoor Prison at Princetown, c.1900, with a convict work-gang - the prison the murderer Selden has escaped from.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Black Tor
pins.story: The granite tor where, one moonlit night, Watson sees a tall, thin figure standing motionless against the sky - the mysterious man on the moor, who is not the enemy at all.

image.caption: A weathered granite tor of the moor (Vixen Tor) - the kind of tor Watson sees the mysterious figure stand upon.
image.credit: From S. Baring-Gould, 'A Book of Dartmoor', 1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Zoological Institute
pins.story: Where it begins and ends. Malone is told by Gladys to go and do something heroic; Challenger, disbelieved at a packed lecture, throws down the challenge; and here at the last the survivors return in triumph, a live pterodactyl produced as proof.

image.caption: “The members of the Exploring Expedition” - the faked frontispiece of the 1912 first edition: Conan Doyle himself, false-bearded, seated as Professor Challenger.
image.credit: Frontispiece, The Lost World (Hodder & Stoughton, 1912) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Para
pins.story: The Amazon's mouth, where the liner lands and the party spends a week assembling stores, guns and the boatmen who will carry them up-country - among them Gomez, a guide whose brother Lord John once shot on the Putumayo.

image.caption: The waterfront of Belém do Pará, the Amazon-mouth port, early 20th century - sailing craft crowding the Ver-o-Peso canal.
image.credit: Álbum ‘Belém da Saudade’, c.1900–12 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Manaos
pins.story: The rubber-boom city fifteen hundred kilometres up the Amazon, at the meeting of the waters. Here the party lodges at a trader's fazenda and waits - until Challenger, who has travelled out in secret, reveals himself and takes command.

image.caption: Manáos on the Rio Negro, watercolour, 1868 - a generation before the rubber boom made it rich; the cathedral rises unfinished above the riverside huts.
image.credit: Unknown artist, Coleção Brasiliana Iconográfica · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: a hidden tributary
pins.story: Beyond Manaus the map goes dark on purpose. A chartered launch, the Esmeralda, carries them days up the great river and into a side-stream 'little smaller than the main stream' - the way to the plateau, kept secret so no one may follow.

pins.name: the last river village
pins.realName: the upper Rio Branco, near Boa Vista
pins.story: The last inhabited place. They beach the launch, send it back to Manaus, and go on by canoe and on foot into empty country - poling up a hidden reed-screened stream, then a nine-day march across morass and bamboo to the foot of the red cliffs.

pins.name: the pinnacle
pins.story: The one way up. They climb the detached pinnacle, fell its summit tree so it drops across the gulf as a bridge, and cross one by one onto Maple White Land - and minutes later Gomez, one of their guides, avenging his brother, topples the bridge into the abyss. There is no way back.

image.caption: ‘General view of the plateau’ - a wash drawing from the 1912 first edition, signed by the narrator, ‘E. D. Malone’.
image.credit: The Lost World (Hodder & Stoughton, 1912) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Maple White Land
pins.realName: Maple White Land (modelled on Mount Roraima)
pins.story: An island in the air, ringed by thousand-foot cliffs, where the prehistoric world survived: iguanodons in the glades, a pterodactyl swamp, ape-men on the heights, a lake of strange beasts. The heart of the book, and a place from which the only escape is a hidden cave down through the rock.

image.caption: Mount Roraima rising behind the forest - the tepui Doyle drew Maple White Land from; hand-coloured aquatint, 1840.
image.credit: After Charles Bentley, from Robert Schomburgk's 'Twelve Views in the Interior of Guiana' (Ackermann, 1840) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Marseilles
pins.story: Where it begins and, for a while, ends: the Old Port and the look-out at Notre-Dame de la Garde; the Catalans and La Réserve, where Dantès is betrothed to Mercédès and, at the feast, arrested on Danglars's forged denunciation.

image.caption: The Vieux-Port at Marseilles, painted by Alphonse Moutte in 1876 - the working harbour that is Edmond Dantès's home, and the quay he is torn from on the day of his arrest.
image.credit: Alphonse Moutte, Déchargement d'un brick à Marseille (1876) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Château d'If
pins.story: The island fortress off Marseille where Dantès is walled up without trial for fourteen years - and where the Abbé Faria, tunnelling in error into his cell, gives him an education, a purpose, and the secret of the treasure buried on Monte Cristo.

image.caption: The Château d'If off Marseilles, drawn by the American painter Miner Kilbourne Kellogg around 1850 - the island fortress where Dantès is buried alive for fourteen years, and from which he escapes into the sea.
image.credit: Miner Kilbourne Kellogg, Château d'If (c.1850) · Smithsonian American Art Museum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the isle of Tiboulen
pins.story: The bare islet, a league from the prison, that Dantès reaches after being flung into the sea sewn in the dead Faria's shroud - the first ground of his freedom, where smugglers take him up.

pins.name: the Isle of Monte Cristo
pins.story: The uninhabited rock in the Tuscan archipelago where Faria's fortune lies hidden in a cave - and, at the last, the isle Dantès withdraws to and sails from. It gives him his new name and his new self.

pins.name: Rome
pins.story: Where the Count of Monte Cristo first appears in society, at the Carnival - mysterious, magnificent, saving young Albert de Morcerf from bandits and so buying his way into the Paris the boy's father inhabits. Later, the net closes here on the last of the enemies.

image.caption: The Piazza del Popolo, painted by Ippolito Caffi in 1847 - the Roman square where the Count makes his entrance during Carnival, and where Franz and Albert watch the crowds pour towards the Corso.
image.credit: Ippolito Caffi, Piazza del Popolo (1847) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Paris
pins.story: The stage of the revenge: the Count's house on the Champs-Élysées and his second at Auteuil, where Villefort's buried secret waits. Here his three enemies have risen high - a banker, a peer, a prosecutor - and here, one by one, he brings them down.

image.caption: The Place de la Concorde and the Champs-Élysées around 1840 - the grand avenue on which the Count takes a house to begin his patient reckoning with the men who ruined Edmond Dantès.
image.credit: Anonymous, the Place de la Concorde and the Champs-Élysées (c.1836–54) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Yanina
pins.story: The court of Ali Pasha in Epirus, where Haydée is a princess - until the French officer Fernand, taken into Ali's trust, betrays him to the Turks for gold. Her father is killed; the crime, twenty years buried, is the thread the Count pulls to destroy the Count de Morcerf.

image.caption: The citadel of Yanina seen across its lake, drawn by Louis Dupré in 1827 - Ali Pasha's city, and the scene of the betrayal by Fernand Mondego that shadows the whole novel.
image.credit: Louis Dupré, The palace and castle of Ioannina seen from the lake (1827) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Constantinople
pins.story: Where the child Haydée and her mother are sold into slavery after Yanina falls - and where, years on, the Count of Monte Cristo finds and buys her, and with her the proof of Fernand's treason.

image.caption: Constantinople and the Bosphorus at sunset, painted by Ivan Aivazovsky in 1856 - the eastern capital through which Haydée passes into slavery, and out of it, at the Count's hand.
image.credit: Ivan Aivazovsky, View of Constantinople and the Bosphorus (1856) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the East
pins.story: No fixed harbour - the open sea toward the Levant, where the white sail of the Count's yacht dwindles at the last, carrying Edmond and Haydée away from the world that made and unmade him.

pins.name: the Bertaux
pins.story: Charles Bovary, a dull widower country doctor, rides out before dawn to set old Rouault's broken leg and meets the farmer's daughter, Emma. He finds excuses to keep returning; when his first wife dies, he courts and marries Emma here.

image.caption: A Pays de Caux farmstead - thatched cob, cart-shed, a woman with the milk-pails - of the kind the invented Bertaux would have been, photographed about 1910.
image.credit: Ferme cauchoise, c.1910 · Bibliothèque nationale de France · public domain

pins.name: Tostes
pins.story: Charles's first practice and the couple's first home. Emma decorates, waits for the passion the novels promised her, and sinks into boredom and a nervous illness of longing - dreaming of Paris among the flower-vases and Pompadour clocks. Her listlessness is what finally moves them away.

image.caption: Tôtes - the novel's Tostes - on the old Rouen–Dieppe coaching road, in a watercolour by Henri Rivière of 1925; later than the story, but the village was little changed.
image.credit: Henri Rivière watercolour, 1925 · Bibliothèque nationale de France · public domain

pins.name: Vaubyessard
pins.story: Emma and Charles attend a single aristocratic ball. She waltzes with a viscount and glimpses real wealth and elegance for the only time in her life - and comes away permanently poisoned. The memory of Vaubyessard becomes the yardstick that makes everything real, Charles and Tostes and Yonville alike, unbearably shabby.

image.caption: A grand Norman château of the period - the Château de Navarre, near Évreux - standing in for the invented Vaubyessard and the one aristocratic night that poisons Emma with longing.
image.credit: 19th-century painting of the Château de Navarre · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Yonville-l'Abbaye
pins.story: The Bovarys' second and final home - Homais's pharmacy, the Lion d'Or inn, the young clerk Léon, and the whole machinery of Emma's ruin: the affair with Rodolphe, the mounting debts to Lheureux the draper, and, at the last, her suicide by the arsenic from Homais's shelves.

image.caption: The single main street of Ry - the real village behind Yonville-l'Abbaye - with its Hôtel de France, photographed about 1900; little changed from Emma's day.
image.credit: Louis Feuquière photograph, c.1900 · Bibliothèque nationale de France · public domain

pins.name: La Huchette
pins.story: Home of Rodolphe Boulanger, a wealthy, practised seducer. He picks Emma out at the Yonville agricultural show, seduces her on a ride in the woods, and carries the affair on here and in her own garden - until he jilts her, by letter, on the very eve of the elopement they had planned.

pins.name: Rouen
pins.story: Emma's nearest taste of the wider world. At the opera she re-meets the clerk Léon; at the cathedral the affair reignites, and the closed cab drives nowhere for hours while it is consummated. Thereafter she comes every Thursday, on the Hirondelle, for 'piano lessons' that are really Léon and the Hôtel de Boulogne - and the debts that destroy her.

image.caption: The west front of Notre-Dame de Rouen - where the cab ride begins - photographed in 1881, its central spire still under scaffolding.
image.credit: Neurdein photograph, 1881 · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Marygreen
pins.story: Jude's boyhood village on the Berkshire downs, his great-aunt Drusilla's home. Here the schoolmaster Phillotson leaves for Christminster, planting the dream that will govern Jude's life.

pins.name: the Brown House
pins.realName: The Brown House, on the Ridgeway
pins.story: The ridge above Marygreen where the boy Jude climbs to catch, on a clear evening, the far-off glow of Christminster's lights - his first sight of the city he will spend his life reaching for.

pins.name: Alfredston
pins.story: The market town five miles north of Marygreen where Jude is apprenticed to a stonemason and learns the craft that will keep him all his life - and never buy him past the college gates.

pins.name: Cresscombe
pins.story: Arabella Donn's home, where she catches Jude's eye across the brook and traps him into a marriage that will shadow the rest of his life.

pins.name: Christminster
pins.story: The university city Jude worships and can never enter. He carves its very stones as a mason while its colleges bar him for his class; he returns to it again and again, and dies within sound of its festival.

image.caption: Christminster is Oxford: the High Street and the college spires, photochrom of the 1890s
image.credit: Photoglob Zürich / Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress (ppmsc.08764) · public domain

pins.name: Lumsdon
pins.story: The village outside Christminster where Phillotson teaches and takes Sue on as his assistant - the schoolroom where she and Jude grow close under the older man's eye.

pins.name: Melchester
pins.story: The cathedral city where Sue trains as a teacher and Jude, following her, works on the cathedral stone. Here she flees the college over the fields to him, and here their doomed closeness deepens.

image.caption: Melchester is Salisbury: the cathedral and spire from the river, photochrom of the 1890s
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress (LCCN 2002708088) · public domain

pins.name: Shaston
pins.story: The hill town where Sue, married to Phillotson, teaches beside him and cannot bear his touch. From here she asks his leave to go to Jude - and, astonishingly, he grants it.

pins.name: Aldbrickham
pins.story: The town where Jude and Sue set up house, unmarried, and try to be let alone. Respectable society will not have them; the whispering drives them out onto the road.

pins.name: Kennetbridge
pins.story: The fair town where the fallen family scrape a living - Sue selling the little spiced 'Christminster cakes' Jude bakes, the dream reduced to a stall of pastry replicas of the colleges.

pins.name: Stoke-Barehills
pins.story: The great agricultural show where Jude and Sue, briefly happy among the crowds, are watched by Arabella - who reads in their tenderness the ruin she can help along.

pins.name: Leddenton
pins.story: Where Phillotson's old friend Gillingham lives, the sober voice that warns him against letting Sue go - the friend who watches him ruin his career for a principle.

pins.name: London
pins.story: Where Arabella washes up between her marriages, keeping a Lambeth tavern - and the port from which she once emigrated, and to which she returns to close in on Jude again.

pins.name: Australia
pins.story: The far country Arabella emigrates to with her family when the marriage to Jude collapses - out of the story for years, until she returns, widowed and harder, to find him again.

pins.name: Marlott
pins.story: Tess's birthplace in the Vale of Blackmoor: the May club-walking where a young Angel Clare, passing on a walking tour, dances - though not with her. She comes home to it in trouble twice, and her father's grand unpaid-for headstone ends up in its churchyard.

image.caption: The Vale of Blackmoor, Dorset - the green vale Hardy renamed Blackmoor and set Marlott in - in a watercolour of about 1913
image.credit: Edward Francis Wells (1876–1952) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Shaston
pins.story: The hill-town on the Vale's rim, shining in Tess's childhood evenings like a row of lamps. It is her gateway out of Blackmoor: the Chaseborough carrier's van starts from here.

pins.name: Trantridge, and The Slopes
pins.story: The Slopes, the spick-and-span house of the Stoke-d'Urbervilles: northern money wearing a bought name. Tess comes to claim kin with people who are no kin at all, keeps Mrs d'Urberville's poultry, and is taught to whistle to bullfinches under Alec's supervision.

pins.name: Chaseborough
pins.story: The decayed market-town where the Trantridge work-folk dance and drink away their Saturday nights. On a September fair-night Tess waits too long for company on the walk home, and accepts a lift from the wrong man.

pins.name: The Chase
pins.story: The oldest wood in England - primaeval yews and oaks, Druidical mistletoe. Riding home in fog, Alec loses the way here not quite honestly; what happens under the trees Hardy keeps in darkness, and it is the night that breaks Tess's life in two.

pins.name: Talbothays Dairy
pins.realName: The Frome valley, near West Stafford
pins.story: Dairyman Crick's great dairy in the Valley of the Great Dairies, where Tess rallies. The heart of the book's one happy stretch: the dawn milkings, the garden at dusk, and Angel Clare - by harvest she has agreed, against her own conscience, to marry him.

image.caption: The water-meadows of the Frome - Hardy's Valley of the Great Dairies, where Talbothays is set - with Dorchester beyond, in a watercolour of about 1903
image.credit: "Picturesque Dorset" postcard series, c.1903 · Newberry Library via CARLI · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Wellbridge
pins.story: The honeymoon house, once a d'Urberville manor: the wedding-night confessions, the dreadful ancestral portraits on the stair, and Angel's sleepwalk with Tess in his arms to a stone coffin in the abbey ruins. The marriage lasts three days here.

image.caption: The ruins of Bindon Abbey at Wool - the abbey by Wellbridge where the sleepwalking Angel lays Tess in a stone coffin - in a pen-and-wash drawing of 1791
image.credit: James Moore, 1791 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

pins.name: Kingsbere-sub-Greenhill
pins.story: The ancestral parish, with rows of d'Urbervilles in the church vaults. Evicted from Marlott on Old Lady-Day, the family finds its promised rooms already let and sets up the four-post bedstead against the church wall, beneath the d'Urberville Window.

image.caption: Bere Regis - Hardy's Kingsbere - in a watercolour of 1910
image.credit: E. W. Haslehust, from The Heart of Wessex, 1910 · public domain

pins.name: Casterbridge
pins.story: The county town at the edge of Tess's map: it is on the dark road to its Saturday market, with the beehives, that the mail-cart spears Prince the horse - the small disaster that sets the whole story moving.

image.caption: Dorchester - Hardy's Casterbridge - seen across the water-meadows, photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Emminster
pins.story: The Clare vicarage, Angel's home. Tess walks fifteen miles here in midwinter to ask his parents for help, and turns back at the gate - her nerve finished off by his brothers' talk on the road, and by the confiscation of her hidden walking-boots.

image.caption: A church and its churchyard at Beaminster - Hardy's Emminster, home of the Clare vicarage - photographed in 1898
image.credit: Valentine & Sons, 1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Evershead
pins.story: The halfway village on Tess's great midwinter walk: breakfast at a cottage by the church going out, a sadder halt coming back - and, a little beyond, the barn where the voice of the preaching ranter is one she knows.

pins.name: Flintcomb-Ash
pins.story: The starve-acre farm: swede-hacking in the rain, reed-drawing in snow, the relentless threshing-machine, all under a farmer with a grudge. Alec d'Urberville - newly converted, then swiftly unconverted - lays siege to her here through the winter.

pins.name: Sandbourne
pins.story: The glittering new pleasure-city where Alec keeps Tess at The Herons, and where Angel finds her too late. Within the hour of his leaving, Alec lies dead in the upstairs room, and Tess is gone after her husband.

image.caption: Bournemouth - Hardy's Sandbourne - the pier and the front seen from the East Cliff, photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bramshurst Court
pins.story: An empty furnished mansion with the blinds down and grass growing on the drive: five days of borrowed married happiness - the only ones Tess and Angel are given - until a caretaker's morning round sends them north again.

image.caption: A glade in the New Forest, Hampshire - the country the fugitives cross to reach the empty house of Bramshurst Court - photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Melchester
pins.story: The fugitives pass through at midnight, keeping off the pavements so their footsteps will not echo, the cathedral rising dimly on their left hand - lost on them now.

image.caption: Salisbury - Hardy's Melchester - the cathedral spire rising over the city the fugitives pass through by night, photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Stonehenge
pins.story: The heathen temple on the open plain, met blind in the dark. Tess sleeps on a sun-warmed slab among the pillars while Angel keeps watch; the police close in with the dawn, and she is ready - 'It is as it should be.'

image.caption: Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain - where Tess sleeps on a sun-warmed slab and is taken at dawn - photographed about 1875
image.credit: Francis Frith (1822–1898) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Wintoncester
pins.story: The old capital of Wessex, where Tess is tried and hanged. Angel and 'Liza-Lu climb the great West Hill and watch, hand in hand, as the black flag moves slowly up the prison tower.

image.caption: Winchester Cathedral - Hardy's Wintoncester, the city of the trial and the hanging - photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the convent of the Holy Lime-Tree
pins.realName: The convent of the Holy Lime-Tree (Święta Lipka), Prussia
pins.story: Where the curse comes to rest and Medardus is born. His father, doing penance for a mortal sin on a pilgrimage north out of Italy, dies here; the child is born and stays his first months, marked from the cradle as the promised, cursed son.

pins.name: the Cistertian Monastery of Kreuzberg
pins.realName: The Cistercian convent of Kreuzberg, on the Saale
pins.story: Medardus's childhood home from the age of four, beside the convent his grandmother the Abbess presides over and his widowed mother serves. Here his piety - and the family's watchful, doomed love - are formed.

pins.name: Königswald
pins.story: Where Medardus takes his vows and grows into a celebrated preacher - and where, guarding the relic of St Anthony, he drinks the Devil's elixir, is unchained, and flees the cloister into the world. He returns here, at the last, to do his penance and die.

pins.name: the devil's chair
pins.realName: The Devil's Abyss, in the Thuringian mountains
pins.story: The hinge of the whole book. Medardus finds Count Victorin asleep on the rock-ledge above the chasm; a cry, a fall, and Victorin is gone into the gulf - and Medardus takes his very name, and the letters at his side, and walks on as another man.

pins.name: the Castle of the Baron von F----
pins.realName: The Baron's castle, in the Thuringian mountains
pins.story: Passing as the dead Victorin, Medardus enters the Baron's household - repels the scheming Baroness Euphemie, conceives his fatal obsession with the ward Aurelie, murders her mad brother Hermogen with a stiletto, and flees as Euphemie dies of the poison she had meant for him.

pins.name: the Prince's residenz
pins.realName: The princely Residenz (Prince von Rosenthurm's court)
pins.story: Reinvented as the polished 'Herr Leonard', Medardus rises at court - until Aurelie arrives, knows him, and he is arrested for the castle murders. His mad double is dragged in bearing the same cruciform scar and confesses in his place; freed and betrothed to Aurelie, Medardus goes mad on the wedding-eve and stabs her.

pins.name: Rome
pins.story: The curse's source, and the place of penance. Medardus scourges himself in a Roman Capuchin convent, is nearly canonised in error, and resists the returning madness before the Pope - and here, in the painter Francesco's parchment, the whole bloody family history is at last laid bare.

pins.name: the galleys of Toulon
pins.story: Where it begins and nearly ends. Nineteen years at the oar - for stealing a loaf of bread for his sister's children, and for trying to escape. Released in 1815 with a yellow passport that shuts every door; dragged back in 1823, and escaped again.

image.caption: The bagne - the convict-prison and arsenal - on the harbour at Toulon, where Valjean served nineteen years. Period engraving.
image.credit: From 'Histoire des Bagnes' (Zaccone), c.1870s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: D-- (Digne)
pins.story: The turn of the whole book. Turned away everywhere, Valjean is fed and housed by the Bishop - and repays him by stealing the silver. Caught, he is not denounced: the Bishop gives him the silver candlesticks too, and buys his soul for good.

image.caption: Digne below its Alpine crags, 1838 - the town where the Bishop's candlesticks remake Valjean.
image.credit: After Rauch, 'Guide pittoresque', 1838 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: M-- sur M--
pins.story: Valjean remade: under a false name he grows rich, turns the town prosperous, and is made its mayor. Here Fantine works in his factory and is ruined; here he confesses his true name to save a stranger, and here he is taken again.

image.caption: The abbey church of Montreuil-sur-Mer, drawn c.1815 - 'Father Madeleine's' town in the north.
image.credit: A. L. Goblain, c.1815 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Arras
pins.story: The court where an innocent man stands accused of being Jean Valjean. Through a sleepless night Valjean fights the temptation to let him hang for it - then races here by cabriolet and, in open court, gives himself up: 'I am Jean Valjean.'

pins.name: Montfermeil, near Paris
