pins.story: The inn where the child Cosette is kept as a drudge by the cruel Thénardiers, and where Valjean, keeping his promise to her dying mother, buys her freedom and leads her away into the dark toward Paris.

image.caption: Valjean leads Cosette away from the Thénardiers' inn at Montfermeil - an illustration of the scene by Alphonse de Neuville, 1865.
image.credit: Alphonse de Neuville, 1865 · Maison de Victor Hugo (Paris Musées) · CC0

pins.name: the Gorbeau hovel
pins.story: Valjean and Cosette's first Paris hiding place, a decayed tenement on the southern edge of the city - until Javert, who has picked up the scent, closes in one winter night.

pins.name: the convent of the Petit-Picpus
pins.story: Escaping Javert over a wall, Valjean carries Cosette into a convent, where the old gardener he once saved takes them in. For five hidden years the hunted man and the child live safe behind its walls, she at school, he as the under-gardener.

pins.name: the Rue Plumet
pins.realName: the house in the Rue Plumet
pins.story: The quiet garden house where Cosette grows into a young woman - and where, through the railings, the student Marius sees her, and the two fall silently, wholly in love, as Paris drifts toward revolt.

pins.name: the barricade of the Rue de la Chanvrerie
pins.realName: the barricade, Rue de la Chanvrerie
pins.story: The barricade of the June 1832 rising, where the students of the ABC and their friends fight and die for a republic. Marius comes here to die for love; Valjean comes to save him; and here Valjean holds the life of his captured hunter, Javert, in his hands - and lets him go.

image.caption: The June 1832 rising - the barricade and its dead. Wood engraving, 1870, of the event Hugo sets his students' last stand in.
image.credit: Pierre Édouard Frère (after Breval), 1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Grand Sewer of Paris
pins.story: The nightmare passage. With the barricade fallen and Marius shot but breathing, Valjean carries the unconscious young man on his back for miles through the filth and dark of the Paris sewers, and comes out at last on the bank of the Seine.

image.caption: The Paris sewers, photographed by Nadar in 1861 - the vaulted tunnels through which Valjean carries the wounded Marius.
image.credit: Nadar (Félix Tournachon), 1861 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: No. 7, Rue de l'Homme-Armé
pins.story: Where the story ends. His secret told and Cosette married to Marius, Valjean withdraws to a bare room to die, believing he has lost her. She and Marius learn the truth and reach him at the last - and by the light of the Bishop's candlesticks, at peace, he dies.

image.caption: A narrow lane of old Paris - Charles Marville photographed the medieval streets just before Haussmann swept them away; the kind of quarter where Valjean lives out his last days.
image.credit: Charles Marville, c.1865 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Seine at the Pont au Change
pins.story: Javert's end. Having lived his whole life for the letter of the law, and been shown mercy by the very man he hunted, the inspector cannot reconcile the two. He walks to the parapet in the dark and gives himself to the river.

image.caption: The Pont au Change and the Tour Saint-Jacques by the Châtelet - the bridge where Javert gives himself to the Seine.
image.credit: Engraving after Hoffbauer, c.1875 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the tower
pins.realName: The Martello tower, Sandycove (now the James Joyce Tower)
pins.story: Stephen lodges in the round granite tower with the mocking Buck Mulligan and the Englishman Haines. Stung by Mulligan's jokes about his dead mother and by his sponging, Stephen hands over the key and resolves not to come back.

image.caption: The Martello tower at Sandycove - now the James Joyce Tower and Museum - where Stephen and Buck Mulligan lodge. No period photograph of it is in the public domain, so this is a present-day view (2025); the granite tower itself stands essentially unchanged since 1904.
image.credit: Karlunun, 2025 · Wikimedia Commons · CC0 (public domain dedication)

pins.name: the school
pins.story: Stephen takes a listless history lesson, helps a struggling boy with his sums, then collects his pay and a windy, anti-Semitic lecture from the headmaster Deasy, who hands him a letter about cattle disease to place with newspaper contacts.

image.caption: Killiney and Dalkey, Co. Dublin, c.1900 - the leafy coastal suburb where Mr Deasy's school is set; the exact schoolhouse on Dalkey Avenue is not pictured.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1900 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Sandymount strand
pins.story: Stephen walks the tide-line alone, thinking in dense inward monologue about sight and time, his family, his lost years in Paris and his mother's death. Almost nothing happens outside his head; it is pure mind against the sea.

pins.name: 7 Eccles street
pins.story: Bloom makes breakfast, buys a kidney from the butcher, brings Molly her tea and post - including the note from Boylan he pretends not to notice. At day's end he brings Stephen home for cocoa, climbs in over the railings, and gets into bed beside a wakeful Molly, who has the last word.

pins.name: Sweny's in Lincoln place
pins.story: Idling through the morning under the alias 'Henry Flower', Bloom collects a flirtatious letter from a secret pen-friend, steps into a church service, and buys the bar of lemon soap that becomes his talisman for the whole day, before heading to the baths.

image.caption: College Green and the front of Trinity College, c.1900 - a few hundred yards from Sweny's chemist in Lincoln Place; a jaunting car and a tram wait in the road.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1900 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the house of mourning
pins.realName: Paddy Dignam's house, Newbridge Avenue, Sandymount
pins.story: The mourners gather to bury Paddy Dignam, dead of drink. Bloom rides in the fourth carriage with Martin Cunningham, Jack Power and Stephen's father Simon Dedalus, quietly the outsider among them.

pins.name: Glasnevin
pins.story: At the graveside Bloom broods on death, on his father's suicide and his infant son Rudy, and on the plain fact of the body in the ground. The other mourners keep him gently at arm's length.

pins.name: the Freeman
pins.realName: The Freeman's Journal & Evening Telegraph offices, Prince's Street
pins.story: Bloom tries to place an advertisement amid the clatter and gusts of the printworks. Stephen arrives with Deasy's letter about cattle, and the men trade rhetoric and gossip. The two protagonists are in the same building within the same hour and never properly meet.

pins.name: Davy Byrne's
pins.story: Sickened by the sight of men wolfing their food in the Burton, Bloom retreats to Davy Byrne's 'moral pub' for a gorgonzola sandwich and a glass of burgundy, thinking of food, of Molly, and of the four o'clock that is coming.

pins.name: the national library
pins.realName: The National Library of Ireland, Kildare Street
pins.story: In the librarian's office Stephen spins his elaborate theory that Shakespeare is the ghost in Hamlet, not the prince, sparring with the poet AE and the librarians. Bloom passes through on his own errand - and on the steps, leaving, comes physically between Stephen and Mulligan.

image.caption: The National Library of Ireland, Kildare Street, in a print of about 1900 - the domed reading room where Stephen holds forth on Shakespeare.
image.credit: Halftone print, c.1900 (from Nordisk familjebok) · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the streets of the city
pins.story: The city itself is the protagonist. A cross-section of Dubliners is followed simultaneously - priests and beggars, Bloom at a bookcart, Stephen meeting his starving sister, and Boylan buying fruit for Molly - all of them bracketed by Church and State, crossing and just missing one another all afternoon.

image.caption: Sackville Street (now O'Connell Street) and O'Connell Bridge, with Nelson's Pillar and the O'Connell monument, photographed c.1900 - the hinge of the city where the day's paths cross, trams and jaunting cars and all.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1900 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the fruit shop
pins.story: Blazes Boylan flirts with the shopgirl while he chooses a basket of fruit and a pot of flowers to be sent ahead to Eccles Street - the courtship gift, dispatched hours before he arrives to keep his appointment with Molly.

image.caption: Grafton Street, Dublin, on a Raphael Tuck 'Oilette' postcard of 1903 - the shopping street where Boylan buys Molly's fruit at Thornton's.
image.credit: Raphael Tuck & Sons 'Oilette' postcard, 1903 · Newberry Library · public domain

pins.name: the Ormond bar
pins.story: In a chapter built like a piece of music, Bloom eats in the Ormond dining room and writes back to his secret pen-friend while, at the bar, two barmaids flirt and Simon Dedalus sings. Bloom knows that at this very hour Boylan's car is jingling north to Molly, and sits with the knowledge.

image.caption: Ormond Quay and Grattan Bridge on the Liffey, c.1898 - the north-bank quay the Ormond Hotel stood on; the hotel itself is out of frame to the right.
image.credit: R. A. H. L'Estrange photograph, c.1898 · Queensland University of Technology · public domain (CC0)

pins.name: Barney Kiernan's
pins.story: An anonymous drinker narrates Bloom's clash with the aggressively nationalist, anti-Semitic 'Citizen'. Bloom stands his ground for tolerance and for his own Jewishness, and escapes in a cab as the Citizen hurls a biscuit-tin after him.

image.caption: Little Britain Street, Dublin, 1899 - the working north-inner-city street on which Barney Kiernan's public house stood.
image.credit: Photograph, 1899 · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the strand
pins.realName: Sandymount Strand, by the Star of the Sea
pins.story: As the summer light fades and a church service drifts across the sand, the young Gerty MacDowell shows herself off on the rocks; Bloom, watching from a distance, quietly gratifies himself as fireworks rise over the bazaar. Then the fancy collapses into his tired, tender thoughts of home.

pins.name: the house of Horne
pins.realName: The National Maternity Hospital, Holles Street
pins.story: Bloom calls to ask after Mina Purefoy, three days in labour, and falls in with Stephen and a rowdy crowd of drunk medical students. As the prose runs through the whole history of English style, the baby is finally born and the crowd spills out towards the pubs. Here Bloom and Stephen truly meet at last.

pins.name: nighttown
pins.story: Written as a hallucinatory play, the night follows the drunk Stephen and a watchful Bloom through the brothels, where guilt and desire erupt as apparitions. When a soldier knocks Stephen down in the street, Bloom stands over him - and in the boy's unconscious face sees a vision of his own dead son.

pins.name: the cabman's shelter
pins.story: In deliberately tired, cliché-clogged prose, Bloom steers the drunk Stephen in for coffee and a bun, fending off a rambling sailor's tall tales and warming, cautiously, to the young man he wants to keep by him.

image.caption: The Custom House and Butt Bridge, where the cabman's shelter stood - photographed while the earlier swing bridge was still in place (1879–91), a little before Bloom's night, and before the Loop Line viaduct.
image.credit: Photograph c.1880s · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Wonder House, and the Zam-Zammah gun
pins.story: Where Kim, astride the great gun, meets the old lama at the museum door and becomes his chela - and where the horse-trader Mahbub Ali hands him a message that draws him toward the Game.

image.caption: The Zam-Zammah gun before the Lahore Museum - 'the Wonder House' - late 19th century; the great bronze piece on which Kim sits at the story's opening.
image.credit: Photographer unknown, late 19th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Umballa
pins.story: Where Kim slips the coded message to the Colonel and, hidden, overhears the cold machinery of the Great Game begin to turn.

image.caption: St Paul's Church in the Ambala cantonment, c.1868 - a period view of the garrison town where Kim carries Mahbub Ali's message to the Colonel.
image.credit: Bourne & Shepherd, c.1868 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Grand Trunk Road
pins.story: The 'river of life' down which Kim and the lama walk among all the peoples of India - and where the army finds Kim, a soldier's son, and takes both the road and the lama from him.

pins.name: Kashi (Benares)
pins.story: Where the lama waits in a Jain temple, and where Kim, grown and half-trained for the Survey, comes down the country to find his master again.

image.caption: The ghats of Benares on the Ganges, 1890s - the holy city where the lama waits among the Jain priests, and to which Kim comes down the country to find him.
image.credit: Photographer unknown, 1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Nucklao - St Xavier's
pins.story: Where Kim spends three years being made a 'Sahib' against his heart, slipping back to the road at every holiday and counting the days until he is free of the classroom.

image.caption: The Chattar Manzil across the Gomti at Lucknow, 1895 - the domes and 'gilt umbrellas' of the book's own described skyline, the city of Kim's schooling.
image.credit: G. W. Lawrie & Co., 1895 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Simla
pins.story: Where, in the holidays, Kim is quietly groomed for the Great Game - the jewel-memory drills of Lurgan Sahib - as the machinery closes its hand around him.

image.caption: Simla, the Raj's summer capital scattered on its wooded ridge, 1850s - where Kim is quietly trained for the Game in the school holidays.
image.credit: Unknown photographer, 1850s · Metropolitan Museum of Art · CC0

pins.name: behind Saharunpore
pins.story: The old woman's house among the fruit-trees, where the travellers rest on the road to the hills - and where, at the very end, the lama's River of the Arrow breaks forth at their feet.

pins.name: the high hills
pins.story: The high country where the old lama, born to the mountains, grows strong again, and stretches his hands toward the snows of the horizon.

image.caption: The high snows and glaciers of the Sutlej Himalaya, 1863 - the horizon of Kinnaur the old lama stretches his hands toward, near the end of his Search.
image.credit: Samuel Bourne, 1863 · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Shamlegh
pins.story: The mountain village where a foreign agent strikes the lama and tears his sacred drawing of the Wheel, and where the Woman of Shamlegh shelters the travellers and helps them away.

image.caption: Chini (Kalpa) above the Sutlej, 1866 - a real Kinnaur village standing for invented Shamlegh-under-the-Snow, perched above the world near the old Tibetan border.
image.credit: Samuel Bourne, 1866 · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: West Vemminghög
pins.story: The flat Scanian farm where an idle boy torments a tomte, is shrunk to thumb-size, and is swept off after the wild geese - and where, one November, he lands again and is made whole.

image.caption: A Scanian farm cottage among its trees, drawn 1924 - an indicative view of the humble southern farmland where Nils begins, and ends.
image.credit: Ferdinand Boberg, 1924 · Nordiska museet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Vittskövle
pins.story: The meadow by Vittskövle manor where the wild geese roost and Akka first weighs the tame gander and the useless boy - and where the fox Smirre makes his first raid.

image.caption: Vittskövle castle, engraved 1680 - the moated Renaissance manor by whose meadows the wild flock first lights.
image.credit: After Gerhard von Burman, 1680 (published by Abraham Fischer, 1756) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Glimminge castle
pins.story: The grim old keep where storks nest on the roof and grey rats have overrun the halls, until Nils pipes the whole horde of them out of the gates and away.

image.caption: Glimmingehus, engraved 1680 - the great medieval stone keep from which Nils pipes the rats.
image.credit: After Gerhard von Burman, 1680 (published by Abraham Fischer, 1756) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Kullaberg
pins.story: The headland where, once a year, the animals keep a truce to watch the grey cranes dance - and where Smirre the fox breaks the peace, and is cast out for it.

image.caption: The lighthouse on Kullen, the Kullaberg cape in Skåne - a 19th-century engraving of the headland where the animals keep their yearly truce.
image.credit: 'Fyrtornet på Kullen i Skåne', 19th-century wood-engraving · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Karlskrona
pins.story: The moonlit naval town where cheeky Nils mocks the bronze king on the square - who climbs down and chases him through the streets, the wooden almsman Rosenbom caught up in the hunt.

image.caption: Stortorget, Karlskrona, 1901 - the great square and the bronze statue of King Karl XI, who climbs down to chase Nils through the streets.
image.credit: Gustav Adolph Johnson, stereograph, 1901 · Riksantikvarieämbetet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Öland's southern point
pins.story: The bare southern heath of Öland, where the flock rests among a great gathering of migrating birds before the leap out over the open Baltic.

pins.name: Visby, and sunken Vineta
pins.story: The walled island town where Nils walks the streets of Vineta, a sunken city risen for one hour in a hundred years, and cannot find the coin that would save it - so it sinks again, and living Visby stands.

image.caption: Visby from the north, watercolour c.1820 - the walled 'city of ruins and roses' on Gotland, set against the legend of drowned Vineta.
image.credit: Lars Cedergren, c.1816–1830 · Riksantikvarieämbetet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Skansen
pins.story: The park where Sweden is gathered in miniature, and where Nils is caught and held; by night the still figures wake, and he wins the friendship of the eagle who will one day carry him back to the geese.

image.caption: Stockholm seen from the heights of Skansen, painted 1889 - the great park just outside the capital where Nils is caught and held.
image.credit: Karl Nordström, 1889 · Nationalmuseum · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Mount Kebnekaise
pins.story: The roof of Sweden, above the glaciers in the Lapland light, where the wild geese come down at last to nest and raise their young through the brief bright summer.

image.caption: Roped climbers on the glacier ice of Kebnekaise, early 20th century - the snow-capped roof of Sweden where the wild geese summer.
image.credit: Ernst Herrmann, c.1900–1940 · Nordiska museet · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Judge Miller's place, in the Santa Clara Valley
pins.story: Buck's soft life: a great, sun-warmed house-dog, king of the ranch - until a gardener with gambling debts steals him and sells him north.

image.caption: Orchard-and-vine country in the Santa Clara Valley - a wood-engraving of the Goodrich farm from a Santa Clara county book of 1888, standing for Judge Miller's place.
image.credit: 'Pen Pictures from the Garden of the World', 1888 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Seattle
pins.story: Unloaded from the express car into a walled yard and 'the man in the red sweater' - Buck's first lesson that a dog stands no chance against a man with a club.

image.caption: The Seattle waterfront from the water, 23 July 1898 - the rush in full spate, and the port Buck was shipped through.
image.credit: L. Heath, 1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Dyea beach
pins.story: Alaskan tidewater, and the shock of the North: the she-dog Curly is pulled down and killed by the pack, and Buck is buckled into the traces for the first time.

image.caption: The Dyea beach at the head of the inlet, c.1898 - the tidewater landing where the stampede came ashore, and Buck met the North.
image.credit: Eric A. Hegg, c.1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the great Chilcoot Divide
pins.story: The killing climb out of the salt water - up the Cañon, past Sheep Camp and the Scales, over the pass that stands between the sea and the frozen interior.

image.caption: The line of stampeders on the 'golden stairs' of the Chilkoot Pass, 1898 - the killing climb over the divide.
image.credit: Eric A. Hegg, 1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the head of Lake Bennett
pins.story: The huge tent camp at the lake's head, where thousands of gold-seekers whipsawed lumber and built boats against the spring break-up - and the sled ran on down the chain of frozen lakes.

image.caption: Whipsawing lumber to build boats at Lake Bennett, 1898 - the camp where thousands waited on the spring break-up.
image.credit: Asahel Curtis, 1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Five Fingers
pins.story: One of the named waters of the frozen Yukon road the mail team runs - the river pinched to four fingers of rock, passed on the ice.

image.caption: The Yukon walled in rock on the long run down to Dawson - a wild reach of the river the mail team ran on the ice.
image.credit: Asahel Curtis, c.1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Dawson
pins.story: The gold capital: journey's end for the dispatch run, and later the saloon where Thornton wagers that Buck can start a thousand-pound sled - and Buck does it.

image.caption: Front Street, Dawson, in the rush (this view 1899) - the gold capital at the end of the trail, dog-team and all.
image.credit: Eric A. Hegg, 1899 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Skaguay (Salt Water)
pins.story: The salt-water end of the endless mail trail. Here the record runs finish; here the worn-out team is at last sold to three green American gold-seekers.

pins.name: the mouth of White River
pins.story: John Thornton's camp, and Buck's rescue: flogged and dying in the traces of Hal's doomed outfit, Buck is cut loose here - moments before the others break through the ice and drown.

image.caption: A Yukon mail dog-team in harness, c.1898 - not White River itself, but the sledding life Buck was broken to, and that ended for him here.
image.credit: Eric A. Hegg, c.1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: a valley where the gold showed like yellow butter
pins.story: The wild heart of the book: gold beyond counting, and no man near. Here the Yeehats kill Thornton, the last tie to man is cut, and Buck answers the call for good - the Ghost Dog at the head of the wolf-pack.

pins.name: New Bedford
pins.story: Where Ishmael, sick of the land, comes to go whaling. At the Spouter-Inn he is made to share a bed with the tattooed Polynesian harpooneer Queequeg - and the two become, in Queequeg's phrase, bosom friends; the friendship is the warm heart of the book. He hears Father Mapple preach the story of Jonah and the whale.

image.caption: New Bedford harbour, crowded with the masts of whaleships, 1867
image.credit: Stephen F. Adams, 1867 · New Bedford Whaling Museum / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Nantucket
pins.story: The lonely sand-island that sends its men to every ocean. Ishmael and Queequeg sign aboard the Pequod - a weathered, bone-decked whaler - while her captain, Ahab, stays unseen in his cabin. On a short, cold Christmas the ship warps out into the wintry Atlantic.

image.caption: Nantucket harbour, c.1908 - a generation after the whaling heyday
image.credit: M. W. Boyer, 1908 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: The Carrol Ground
pins.realName: The South Atlantic grounds (the Carrol Ground, south of St Helena)
pins.story: The long slide south down the Atlantic. Here Ahab at last climbs to the quarter-deck, nails a gold doubloon to the mast, and declares the voyage's true errand - the white whale - swearing the whole crew to the hunt. By night a silvery 'spirit-spout' lures the ship on.

pins.name: The Cape of Good Hope
pins.realName: The Cape of Good Hope, South Africa
pins.story: The Pequod claws round the southern tip of Africa through a black gale - Ahab going the long way east, round the Cape rather than the Horn, to reach the equator in the right season. Storm-ravens perch on the stays; Starbuck watches his captain sleep bolt upright over his charts.

image.caption: Cape Town from Table Bay - the landfall a whaler saw rounding the Cape; watercolour, 1867
image.credit: Lt James Henry Butt, 1867 · Royal Museums Greenwich / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: The Crozet grounds
pins.story: In the cold Indian Ocean the rumours of Moby Dick begin to gather. Three ships are spoken here: the weather-bleached Goney, whose hail fails on the wind; the Town-Ho, with 'strong news' of the white whale; and the plague-struck Jeroboam, whose mad prophet Gabriel shrieks a warning against the hunt.

pins.name: The Straits of Sunda
pins.realName: The Straits of Sunda / Java Head
pins.story: Standing in the great gate between Sumatra and Java, the Pequod runs into an immense, panicked herd of whales - the Grand Armada - wheeling in a vast ring, while she herself is chased through the strait by the piratical proas of the Malays. A giant squid has already risen on the run east, pale as a spectre.

pins.name: The Bashee Isles
pins.realName: The Bashee Isles (Batanes), the gate to the Pacific
pins.story: Gliding out through the last of the archipelago, the Pequod comes at last onto the open Pacific. Ishmael salutes the great South Sea with love; Ahab, blind to all of it, fixes only on the Japanese cruising-ground ahead, and the season on the Line beyond.

pins.name: The Japan grounds
pins.story: In the resplendent Japanese seas the direst of storms strikes head-on. In the typhoon the masts burn with St Elmo's fire - 'the Candles' - and Ahab, gripping the flames, defies the corposants; the lightning reverses the compass, and by morning he forges a new needle and points the prow for the equator. Two ships pass: the Samuel Enderby, whose captain lost an arm to Moby Dick and only laughs, and the joy-drunk, homeward-bound Bachelor.

pins.name: The Line
pins.story: The equator at last, where Ahab has willed the whole voyage to end. Two Nantucket ships bring the final omens: the Rachel, which met the whale only yesterday and lost a boat - and the captain's own son - and begs Ahab to help search; he refuses and sails on. Then the Delight, a wrecked boat on her shears and a corpse being sewn into its hammock. The next dawn, the white whale is raised.

pins.name: The wreck
pins.story: The end of the three-day chase. On the third day the white whale turns at last on the ship herself, and smites the Pequod's bow till she founders; Ahab, darting his final harpoon, is caught round the neck by the running line and dragged down with her. The sea closes over. One man is left, floating on a coffin.

pins.name: the Boar's-Head Tavern in Eastcheap
pins.story: The Boar's Head, Falstaff's kingdom of sack and lies. Here Prince Hal idles among thieves and drawers, plays out a mock-interview with his father, and speaks the cold aside that one day he will cast all this off.

image.caption: Falstaff holds forth in the Boar's Head, Eastcheap (1 Henry IV, II.iv); steel engraving, 1825.
image.credit: Robert Smirke R.A., engraved by Charles Heath, 1825 · The Metropolitan Museum of Art · public domain

pins.name: the court; the Jerusalem chamber
pins.story: The court, and the crown that will not rest easy. A careworn Henry IV reproves his truant son, wins his war and grieves his health away, and dies at last in the Jerusalem Chamber as Hal lifts the crown from his pillow.

image.caption: Westminster Abbey and Westminster Bridge from the Thames, with river craft; aquatint, 1810 - four centuries later than the play, like the map beneath it.
image.credit: After Rowlandson & Pugin, from Ackermann's Microcosm of London, 1810 · public domain

pins.name: Gadshill
pins.story: On the old Dover Road, Falstaff and his fellows rob the King's pilgrims at dawn - and are robbed in turn by Hal and Poins in disguise, for the sport of hearing Falstaff lie about it afterward.

image.caption: The carriers and conspirators before dawn at Gad's Hill (1 Henry IV, II.i); painted for the Boydell Shakespeare gallery.
image.credit: Robert Smirke R.A. (1753–1845), Boydell Shakespeare series · Royal Shakespeare Theatre collection · public domain

pins.name: Warkworth. Before the castle
pins.story: The Percy stronghold in the north. Here Hotspur reads a wavering lord's letter and teases his wife Kate before riding to the rising; and here, in the second play, his father hears of his death and is talked into flight.

image.caption: The ruined keep and towers of Warkworth Castle, the Percy stronghold; engraving, 1814.
image.credit: J. Greig after L. Clennell, from The Border Antiquities of England and Scotland, 1814 · public domain

pins.name: Bangor. The Archdeacon's house
pins.story: In Wales the confederates spread a map and carve the kingdom into three - Hotspur quarrelling that the winding Trent has cheated his share of the land, and demanding the river's course be turned.

pins.name: the field near Shrewsbury
pins.story: The field north of the town where the two Harrys meet at last. Hal kills Hotspur in single combat; Falstaff counterfeits death, then rises to claim the kill as his own; and the Percy rebellion breaks.

image.caption: The death of Hotspur at the Battle of Shrewsbury, 1403; colour illustration, 1864.
image.credit: James William Edmund Doyle, A Chronicle of England, 1864 · public domain

pins.name: a public road near Coventry
pins.story: On the march to Shrewsbury, Falstaff halts near Coventry - ashamed to be seen leading his scarecrow company of pressed and ragged men, 'food for powder', up to the field.

image.caption: St Michael's, Coventry - the great spired medieval church by the road Falstaff's ragged company took; watercolour, 1802.
image.credit: William Crotch, 1802 · Norfolk Museums · public domain

pins.name: York. The Archbishop's palace
pins.story: The Archbishop of York takes up the broken rebellion, preaching a commonwealth sick of the king it chose, and marshalling a churchman's war in the north.

image.caption: York, the Minster rising over the city walls - Archbishop Scroop's seat; photochrom, c.1890s.
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Gaultree Forest
pins.realName: Forest of Galtres, north of York
pins.story: In the forest north of York the rebels are met, their grievances granted and their army sent home - and then, disarmed, arrested for treason. The coldest victory in the plays, and won by a broken word.

pins.name: Gloucestershire. Before Justice Shallow's house
pins.story: At Justice Shallow's, Falstaff presses recruits and remembers a wild youth - 'we have heard the chimes at midnight' - then, hearing the old King is dead, posts for London and the favour he is sure awaits him.

image.caption: A Gloucestershire prospect over the Severn vale - an indicative view for Justice Shallow's unlocated house; oil, early 19th century.
image.credit: William Turner of Oxford (1789–1862), 'Newnham-on-Severn from Dean Hill' · Art UK · public domain

pins.name: Geneva
pins.story: Victor's home city, and the still centre the whole book keeps returning to. He is raised here with Elizabeth; here William is murdered and Justine hanged for it; here the family gathers, grieves, and at last plans the wedding.

image.caption: Geneva and the Pont du Mont-Blanc, with the Salève beyond - steel engraving, c.1850
image.credit: Ludwig Rohbock (del.) & Fr. Müller (sc.), pub. G. G. Lange, Darmstadt, c.1850 · Swiss National Library / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Belrive
pins.story: The family's country house on the eastern shore. It is here, as a boy, that Victor watches lightning shatter an old oak and first turns his mind to the forces that will undo him; here too the household retreats after Justine's death.

image.caption: A villa on the shore of Lake Geneva, the Alps beyond - steel engraving 'Lake of Geneva', 1840
image.credit: William Miller after D. McKenzie, 1840 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Plainpalais
pins.story: The open ground just outside the old city walls where little William is found strangled - and where, in a flash of lightning that night, Victor first glimpses the creature he made and knows at once who the murderer is.

pins.name: Chamounix
pins.story: The Alpine valley Victor flees to in his grief. From here he climbs to Montanvert, hoping the sublime scenery will quiet his mind - and instead finds the creature waiting for him on the ice.

image.caption: The Mer de Glace above the Chamonix valley - photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Montanvert
pins.story: The glacier viewpoint above Chamonix, over the great river of ice. Here the creature bounds across the crevasses to meet its maker face to face, and - for the length of nine chapters - tells its own story: the cottage, the rejection, the murder of William.

image.caption: The Mer de Glace from Montanvert, with the hut on the moraine - hand-coloured view, c.1781
image.credit: Carl Ludwig Hackert, c.1781 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Evian
pins.story: The little town on the far shore of Lake Geneva where Victor and Elizabeth stop on their wedding night. Sure the creature's threatened vengeance is meant for him, Victor keeps watch - and hears Elizabeth scream in the room he has left her in.

image.caption: Évian-les-Bains from the lake, the mountains behind - photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photoglob Zürich / Photochrom Print Collection, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Ingolstadt
pins.story: The Bavarian university where Victor studies natural philosophy and, on a dreary night of November, gives his creature life - then flees the room in horror. When he returns, it is gone. Years later he comes back to buy the instruments for a second, female creature.

image.caption: Ingolstadt from the fields, the Liebfrauenmünster rising over the town - hand-coloured lithograph, 1823
image.credit: Adolph Kunike, 1823 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the cottage of the De Laceys
pins.story: The hovel where the creature shelters, watching a poor blind man and his children through a chink in the wall. Here it learns to speak, to read, to hope for kindness - and, when it finally shows itself, is beaten and driven out. Its rejection here turns it toward revenge.

image.caption: The Bavarian countryside on the Danube, near Ingolstadt - oil painting, 1863
image.credit: Pieter Franciscus Peters, 1863 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Strasburgh
pins.story: Where Victor and Clerval meet to begin their journey to England. From here they take a boat down the Rhine, Victor carrying his chemical instruments and the dread of the work still to do.

image.caption: The old Maison Kammerzell, Strasbourg - photochrom, c.1895
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1895 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Rotterdam
pins.story: The end of the Rhine voyage, on the plains of Holland, where Victor and Clerval take ship across the Channel for England.

image.caption: The Leuvehaven, Rotterdam, thick with sailing barges - photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: London
pins.story: The travellers' point of rest for several months. Clerval delights in the city; Victor, dreading his promise, quietly gathers the materials for the second creature before they turn north.

image.caption: London from the Thames, St Paul's towering over the shipping - oil painting, c.1850
image.credit: John Gendall, c.1850 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Oxford
pins.story: A stop on the leisurely tour north - Windsor, Oxford, Matlock, the lakes. Clerval is happy among the ancient colleges; Victor walks the same streets 'a blasted tree', the bolt already in his soul.

image.caption: Oxford, the High Street and the spires and domes beyond - photochrom, c.1895
image.credit: Photoglob Zürich / Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1895 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Perth
pins.story: Where a Scottish acquaintance expects the two friends. Victor leaves Clerval here to make 'the tour of Scotland alone' - really to finish his loathsome work in solitude - with Perth set as their rendezvous.

image.caption: Perth and the River Tay from Bridgend - photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: one of the remotest of the Orkneys
pins.story: The bleak rock where Victor sets up his laboratory to build the female creature. Half-finished, appalled at what a race of them might do, he tears the thing apart - and looks up to see the creature watching at the window. That night he rows out and sinks her remains at sea, and is carried off course.

image.caption: The Old Man of Hoy, a bare sea-stack among the remotest of the Orkneys, in a Daniell aquatint of 1817 - the kind of desolate rock where Victor sets up his second laboratory.
image.credit: William Daniell, 'The Old Man of Hoy', 1817 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the town where Mr. Kirwin is magistrate
pins.realName: A town on the Irish coast
pins.story: Cast adrift from the Orkneys, Victor drifts to an Irish harbour - where the townsfolk, hostile from the start, accuse him of a murder committed the night before. The body is Clerval's, the fingermarks on its throat the creature's signature. He is imprisoned, falls into fever, and is at last acquitted.

image.caption: The wild west coast of Ireland under a breaking sky - oil painting, 1913
image.credit: Robert Henri, 'West Coast of Ireland', 1913 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: St. Petersburgh
pins.story: Where Walton's letters begin. Full of an explorer's hope of a passage to the pole, he writes to his sister before travelling north to hire a ship and crew.

image.caption: St Petersburg, the Admiralty and the Neva - photochrom, c.1895
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1895 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Archangel
pins.story: The White Sea port where Walton fits out his vessel and gathers his sailors. It is also the northern edge of the world Victor crosses on the creature's trail, before both pass out onto the frozen sea.

image.caption: The White Sea port of Archangel, tall ships at anchor - photograph, c.1900
image.credit: Unknown photographer, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: a vessel bound for the Black Sea
pins.story: Following the creature down the Rhône to the Mediterranean, Victor watches it board a ship for the Black Sea by night and takes passage in the same vessel - the hinge where the pursuit leaves western Europe and turns toward Russia and the ice.

image.caption: Ships on the Black Sea at dusk, painted by Ivan Aivazovsky in 1871 - the sea Victor's creature sails for, taking passage in the same vessel.
image.credit: Ivan Aivazovsky, 1871 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: a wretched hamlet on the seashore
pins.story: The last land. Victor reaches a poor coastal hamlet to learn the creature has stolen a sledge and a drove of dogs and struck out across the sea ice the night before. He exchanges his land-sledge for one built for the Frozen Ocean, stocks it, and follows onto the ice.

image.caption: A hut on a bleak Arctic shore, ice and a ship offshore - 'Coast of Nova Zembla', 1841
image.credit: François-Auguste Biard, 'Côte de la Nouvelle-Zemble', 1841 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the everlasting ices of the north
pins.realName: The frozen sea, toward the Pole
pins.story: Where the two threads finally meet. Walton's ship is frozen fast; out of the white a dog-sledge brings a dying Victor aboard. He tells the whole story and dies; the creature comes to the cabin to mourn its maker, then springs from the window onto an ice raft and is 'lost in darkness and distance'.

image.caption: A ship beset and crushed in the Arctic pack ice by moonlight - oil painting, c.1846
image.credit: W. H. Smyth, 'Perilous Position of HMS Terror, 1837', c.1846 · Royal Museums Greenwich / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the northern coast
pins.story: The north coast of Scotland, where Victor takes a boat out to the remotest of the Orkneys.

image.caption: Thurso across its bay from Holborn Head, in a William Daniell aquatint of c.1820 - the Caithness coast where Victor takes a boat out to the remote Orkneys.
image.credit: William Daniell, from A Voyage Round Great Britain, c.1820 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Havre-de-Grace
pins.story: The French port where Victor and his father land from Ireland, on the road home to Geneva.

image.caption: The port of Le Havre, painted by Claude Monet in 1874 - where Victor and his father land from Ireland on the road home to Geneva.
image.credit: Claude Monet, 'Port of Le Havre', 1874 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the blue Mediterranean
pins.story: Where the Rhone meets the Mediterranean, and Victor takes passage after the creature, bound for the Black Sea.

image.caption: The old port of Marseille, painted by Alphonse Moutte - indicative of the Mediterranean harbour where Victor, following the Rhone to the sea, takes ship after the creature. The novel names only 'the blue Mediterranean', and Moutte's view (1876) is later than Victor's own day.
image.credit: Alphonse Moutte, Déchargement d'un brick à Marseille (1876) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Essendean
pins.story: The quiet parish where David is born and orphaned, and which he leaves one June morning in 1751 with a letter that sends him to claim his inheritance at the House of Shaws.

image.caption: A lonely house on a Lowland brae, c.1890 - an indicative view of the quiet upland country-side of Essendean, David's invented home parish south-west of Edinburgh
image.credit: Robert W. Little, c.1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the house of Shaws
pins.story: The blighted, half-finished house of David's miserly uncle Ebenezer, who first tries to kill him on a broken stair in the dark, then has him kidnapped. David returns at the end to trap the old man into confessing.

image.caption: Borthwick Castle, a gaunt Lowland tower house, engraved in the late 18th century - an indicative stand-in for the grim, half-built house of Shaws, which Stevenson invented but sited near Cramond
image.credit: Engraving by P. Mazell, late 18th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Queen's Ferry
pins.story: The ferry-town on the Forth where Ebenezer walks David down 'on business' and, at the Hawes Inn, has him struck down and carried aboard the Covenant. David returns here at the end to find the lawyer Rankeillor.

image.caption: The Queensferry from the south-east, with the Firth of Forth and a coach on the shore road, engraved 1843
image.credit: William Miller after Clarkson Stanfield, 1843 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Torran rocks
pins.realName: The Torran Rocks, off the Ross of Mull
pins.story: The reef, off the south-west end of Mull near Iona, where the brig Covenant is thrown by the tide and beats herself to pieces - scattering the crew and casting David up alone.

pins.name: Earraid
pins.story: The little tidal islet where David is cast ashore alone after the wreck, and spends wretched days believing himself trapped - until he grasps that the sea goes out and he can simply walk off.

image.caption: The ruins of Iona Cathedral from the north-west, c.1860 - Iona lies just off Earraid at the south-west tip of the Ross of Mull, the very corner where David is cast ashore
image.credit: E. J. Bedford, c.1860 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Island of Mull
pins.realName: Isle of Mull (the Ross of Mull)
pins.story: The long, crooked island David treks across on Alan's silver-button token, guided and half-swindled by the folk of the Ross, working east toward the Appin country.

image.caption: Fishing smacks off Staffa in choppy seas, with Iona in the distance, 1853 - the Hebridean waters at the south-west end of Mull where David is wrecked
image.credit: Anthony Vandyke Copley Fielding, 1853 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Kinlochaline / Morven
pins.story: The mainland shore David is ferried to across the Sound of Mull, and the wild peninsula of Morvern he crosses, still following Alan's trail toward Appin.

image.caption: Kinlochaline Castle at the head of Loch Aline in Morvern, painted by Horatio McCulloch in 1855 - the mainland shore where David steps off the ferry from Mull and sets out across the Highlands.
image.credit: Horatio McCulloch, 'Kinlochaline Castle, Morvern', 1855 · Kelvingrove Art Gallery · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Kingairloch / Ardgour
pins.story: The Loch Linnhe shore where David, near the end of his lonely trek, takes a boat across into Appin - and walks straight into the killing that changes everything.

image.caption: Loch Linnhe near Fort William, painted on the spot in 1848 - the deep, still sea-loch David comes down to and is rowed across into Appin
image.credit: Watercolour, 1848 (Rijksmuseum) · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

pins.name: the wood of Lettermore
pins.story: The Appin wood where David, arriving at the worst possible moment, sees the Red Fox shot from the hillside - and, marked as a suspect, falls in with Alan Breck and takes to his heels. Here the flight begins.

image.caption: Glen Coe, in the Appin country near Ballachulish, engraved after Turner in 1836 - the brooding Highland ground of the wood of Lettermore, where the Red Fox, Colin Campbell, is shot dead in the Appin Murder.
image.credit: William Miller after J. M. W. Turner, 'Glen Coe', 1836 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Heugh of Corrynakiegh
pins.story: The hidden cleft in the Appin hills where the two shelter, fish for trout, and signal for money and news, while the redcoats scour the country below for the murderers.

image.caption: A Highland glen and sea-loch in Argyll, 1867 - an indicative view of the Appin hill country where the invented cleft of Corrynakiegh hides the two fugitives
image.credit: Robert S. Duncanson, 1867 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Moor
pins.story: The vast, skeleton-firred bog the fugitives cross by crawling from heather-bush to heather-bush under a blazing sun, in full view of the soldiers on the surrounding hills - the set-piece of the whole flight.

image.caption: The Moor of Rannoch, painted 1868 - the great desolate bog the fugitives crawl across in full view of the soldiers
image.credit: Andrew MacCallum, 1868 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Ben Alder / Cluny's Cage
pins.story: The mountain into whose 'heart' the flight drives them, where Cluny Macpherson's scouts take them up to his wicker hiding-place. Feverish, David quarrels bitterly with Alan over money gambled away.

image.caption: A storm breaking over a Highland mountain pass (Glen Coe), engraved 1836 after Turner - an indicative view of the wild mountain fastness of Ben Alder, into whose heart the flight drives David and Alan
image.credit: William Miller after J. M. W. Turner, 1836 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Balquhidder
pins.story: The glen where David falls sick and the two rest, and where Alan out-plays Robin Oig, one of Rob Roy's sons, on the pipes rather than fight him. When David mends, the flight resumes.

image.caption: November on the Braes of Balquhidder - the MacGregor country where David falls sick and Alan out-pipes a son of Rob Roy
image.credit: Thomas Hunt, 1914 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Bridge of Stirling
pins.story: The guarded bridge that 'bridles the wild Hielandman' - where Alan's ruse fails to get them across, and they must turn east and downstream to steal a passage over the Forth.

image.caption: Stirling Castle on its rock above the Forth, painted c.1840 - the town guarding the bridge that 'bridles the wild Hielandman'
image.credit: Montague Stanley, c.1840 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the clachan of Limekilns
pins.story: The little Forth-side clachan where a change-house girl, taking David for a hunted gentleman, rows them over the water single-handed by night - the quiet, kindly climax of the flight.

pins.name: the British Linen Company's bank
pins.story: Journey's end: David, his inheritance won and Alan parted from him on the Corstorphine road, walks into the city 'to the very doors of the British Linen Company's bank' - rich, and heavy-hearted.

image.caption: Edinburgh Castle above the Grassmarket, photographed c.1865–85 - the Old Town David walks into at journey's end to claim his fortune
image.credit: George Washington Wilson, c.1865–85 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Munich
pins.story: Where the novel's travelling begins: Jonathan leaves by the evening train on 1 May, bound east.

image.caption: Marienplatz, Munich, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1890 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Vienna
pins.story: An early-morning stop on Jonathan's outbound journey - the train already running late.

image.caption: The Ringstrasse and Parliament, Vienna, photographed 1890
image.credit: Victor Angerer, 1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Buda-Pesth
pins.story: Crossing the Danube here, Jonathan feels he is leaving the West and entering the East.

image.caption: The Danube at Budapest, the Chain Bridge and Buda Castle beyond, photographed in the 1890s - the crossing where Jonathan feels he is leaving the West for the East.
image.credit: Fortepan / Budapest City Archives, 1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Klausenburgh
pins.story: Jonathan overnights at the Hotel Royale, dines on paprika hendl, and starts noting recipes for Mina.

image.caption: The main square (Főtér) and St Michael's Church, Kolozsvár (Cluj), c.1890
image.credit: Unknown photographer, c.1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bistritz
pins.story: The Golden Krone Hotel. The landlady begs him not to travel on the eve of St George's Day, and presses her crucifix on him.

image.caption: The Saxon Evangelical church and market square of Bistrița (Bistritz), Transylvania - the town of the Golden Krone Hotel, where the landlady presses her crucifix on Jonathan.
image.credit: K.u.k. Kriegspressequartier, c.1916 · Austrian National Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Borgo Pass
pins.story: The fellow passengers cross themselves as the diligence climbs; at the summit, a calèche with a driver of prodigious strength is waiting.

pins.name: the Count's castle, above the Borgo Pass
pins.story: Jonathan is the Count's guest, then his prisoner - the missing reflection, the three sisters, the crawl down the castle wall. In the final chapter the Count is overtaken and destroyed just below its walls, at the cost of Quincey Morris's life.

image.caption: The Carpathian mountains behind the Count's castle - the Tatra range, painted c.1860
image.credit: Gusztáv Kelety, c.1860 · Herman Ottó Museum · public domain

pins.name: Varna
pins.story: The Demeter sails from here with fifty boxes of earth. Months later the hunters wait here to ambush the returning Count - and the ship slips past them.

image.caption: The old fortress wall and gate at Varna, on the Black Sea, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Bulgarian Archives State Agency (BASA), c.1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Crescent
pins.story: Lucy and her mother take rooms for the summer; Mina joins them. It is from here that Lucy begins walking in her sleep.

image.caption: Whitby harbour and the abbey from the East Cliff, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the churchyard on the East Cliff
pins.story: Lucy and Mina's favourite seat among the graves. On the night of the great storm's aftermath, Mina finds Lucy here at midnight - with something long and black bending over her.

image.caption: St Mary's church and its churchyard on Whitby's East Cliff, photographed c.1890 - Lucy and Mina's favourite seat among the graves, where Mina finds Lucy at midnight.
image.credit: Photochrom, c.1890 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Tate Hill Pier
pins.story: The derelict Demeter drives ashore in the storm, her dead captain lashed to the wheel - and an immense dog leaps from her bow and vanishes up the cliff.

pins.name: King's Cross
pins.story: Fifty boxes of common earth arrive by the Great Northern Railway, to be carted quietly on to Carfax.

image.caption: The Great Northern Railway terminus at King's Cross, engraved 1851
image.credit: Illustrated London News, 1851 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Hospital of St. Joseph and Ste. Mary, Buda-Pesth
pins.realName: Hospital of St. Joseph and Ste. Mary, Budapest
pins.story: Jonathan surfaces here after his escape, six weeks gone with brain fever. Mina travels out to nurse him, and they are married at his bedside.

pins.name: Exeter
pins.story: Mr Hawkins's town, and the Harkers' married home. When Hawkins dies, Jonathan inherits the practice - and the means to fight.

image.caption: The west front of Exeter Cathedral, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1890 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Hillingham
pins.story: The Westenra house. Lucy fades here despite the transfusions of four men; the wolf comes through the window; her mother dies of the shock, and Lucy follows.

pins.name: Carfax
pins.story: The Count's first English lair - the estate Jonathan sold him without knowing it. The hunters' first raid finds the chapel reeking and swarming with rats, and seals its boxes one by one.

pins.name: the lunatic asylum
pins.story: Dr Seward's asylum, next door to Carfax. Renfield catalogues his flies and spiders here, is broken by his master, and dies of it; Mina, sleeping under this roof, is attacked.

pins.name: 347, Piccadilly
pins.story: Jonathan sees the Count in daylight here - grown young. At the house numbered 347 the hunters finally corner him; he bursts through the window and is gone.

image.caption: Piccadilly Circus, London, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the churchyard at Kingstead
pins.story: Lucy's tomb. Van Helsing proves the empty coffin to unbelieving men, and Arthur - her fiancé - drives the stake at last.

image.caption: The Gothic entrance to Highgate Cemetery - Stoker's 'Kingstead' - in a wood engraving of 1838, the burial ground where Van Helsing proves Lucy's empty coffin and Arthur drives the stake.
image.credit: The Mirror of Literature, 1838 · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Hampstead Heath
pins.story: Where the newspapers' 'bloofer lady' lures children after dark, each returned with small wounds at the throat.

image.caption: The Firs on Hampstead Heath at dusk, painted by William James Callcott in 1885 - the ground where the newspapers' 'bloofer lady' lures children after dark.
image.credit: William James Callcott, 1885 · Art UK · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: 197, Chicksand Street, Mile End New Town
pins.story: One of the Count's east-London depots, run to ground by Jonathan's patient detective work and sterilised on the last day in London.

pins.name: Jamaica Lane, Bermondsey
pins.story: The Bermondsey depot south of the river - found, entered and sterilised the same day as Mile End.

pins.name: Doolittle's Wharf
pins.story: The Czarina Catherine takes on a single great box here, cleared for Varna - the Count shipping himself home.

pins.name: Charing Cross
pins.story: The whole company leaves from here on 12 October, racing the Count's ship across a continent.

image.caption: The Eleanor Cross in the yard of Charing Cross station, London, c.1903
image.credit: A. H. Kirk, postcard, c.1903 · New York Public Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Paris
pins.story: One night only: the places secured for them on the Orient Express.

image.caption: The Gare de l'Est in Paris, terminus of the Orient Express, photographed around 1900 - where the party's places east are secured for a single night.
image.credit: Henri Ferrand, c.1900 · Bibliothèque nationale de France · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Amsterdam
pins.story: Van Helsing's home. He shuttles back and forth for his books, his instruments and his garlic.

image.caption: The Herengracht canal in Amsterdam, photographed c.1890 - Van Helsing's home city, which he shuttles back to for his books, his instruments and his garlic.
image.credit: Photochrom, c.1890 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Galatz
pins.story: The ship gives everyone the slip and lands here instead of Varna; the box passes into Szgany hands, and the pursuit splits into three prongs.

pins.name: Veresti
pins.story: Railhead of the first prong: Van Helsing and Mina leave the train here and take a carriage into the snow, making for the Borgo Pass.

pins.name: Miss Pinkerton's academy
pins.story: Miss Pinkerton's genteel academy on the real riverside street of Chiswick Mall, where the book opens: Becky and Amelia are turned out into the world, and Becky flings the presentation dictionary back into the garden.

image.caption: Chiswick Mall from the Thames, 1829 - the riverside terrace where Miss Pinkerton's academy stood, its church and the Red Lion named along the front.
image.credit: From Samuel Leigh's panorama of the Thames, 1829 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Russell Square
pins.story: The Bloomsbury square where the wealthy Sedleys and, facing them, the Osbornes both live - the comfortable world Amelia is born into and loses when her father is ruined, and to which she gives up her son.

pins.name: Queen's Crawley
pins.story: Sir Pitt Crawley's crumbling Hampshire seat, where Becky goes as governess and lays the foundations of her fortune - charming the old baronet and secretly marrying his son Rawdon. Wholly invented; the text places it in Hampshire, near a Southampton doctor.

image.caption: Bramshill House, Hampshire - a great Jacobean seat of the kind imagined for Sir Pitt Crawley's shabby-grand old hall. Indicative; Queen's Crawley is invented.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1818) · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Brighton
pins.story: The seaside resort of George and Amelia's honeymoon, where Becky and Rawdon join them - until Dobbin brings the order that sends the regiment abroad, and the whole party for the war.

image.caption: The Chain Pier at Brighton, painted by John Constable in 1826–27 - the fashionable seafront where Rawdon and Becky take their honeymoon.
image.credit: John Constable, Chain Pier, Brighton (1826–27) · Tate · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Curzon Street
pins.story: Becky and Rawdon's married house in Mayfair, the height of her rise - 'living well on nothing a year', received at Court, the favourite of Lord Steyne - and the scene of the scandal that brings it all down.

pins.name: Fulham
pins.story: The modest cottage where the widowed Amelia lives out her years of genteel poverty with her parents and little Georgy, before the son is given up to old Osborne's wealth.

image.caption: Fulham, etched by James McNeill Whistler c.1879 - the old wooden bridge and All Saints' tower on the Thames, near Amelia's genteel poverty.
image.credit: James McNeill Whistler, Fulham (c.1879) · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Ostend
pins.story: The Belgian port where the packet lands the regiment and its followers, on the road to the war - Ghent and Bruges beyond it, and Brussels at the end.

image.caption: The harbour at Ostend in a squall, Andreas Achenbach, 1858 - the Belgian packet port on the road to Brussels.
image.credit: Andreas Achenbach, The Harbour at Ostend (1858) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Brussels
pins.story: Where the army gathers and the English wait - the Duchess of Richmond's ball on the eve, the guns heard in the night, and Amelia praying through the day of the battle a few miles to the south.

image.caption: The Grand-Place, Brussels, photographed c.1875 - the city filling with English visitors on the eve of Waterloo.
image.credit: Anonymous photograph, c.1875 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the field of Waterloo
pins.story: The field south of Brussels where the battle is fought on 18 June 1815 - and where George Osborne is killed, a bullet through his heart, in the book's most famous line.

image.caption: The field of Waterloo, drawn on the spot in 1816 - the quiet farmland at Mont-Saint-Jean a year after the battle that widows Amelia in a sentence.
image.credit: Robert Hills, from Sketches in Flanders and Holland (1816) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Boulogne
pins.story: The French coast where Becky begins her long Continental exile after the scandal - 'Queen of British Boulogne' for a season, until the old creditors and the old stories find her out and move her on.

image.caption: Boulogne harbour, painted by Richard Parkes Bonington c.1823 - the cheap Channel port the ruined Sedleys and old Osborne retreat to.
image.credit: Richard Parkes Bonington, Boulogne Harbour (c.1823) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Pumpernickel
pins.story: Thackeray's invented little German grand-duchy, with its court and its opera - where Amelia, Dobbin and Jos come to rest at the end, and where a sunk and shabby Becky reappears to close the account. Long identified with Weimar, where Thackeray had stayed.

image.caption: The market place at Weimar, c.1900 - the little German court-town generally taken for Thackeray's Pumpernickel, where the puppets go back into the box. Indicative.
image.credit: Postcard after a Weimar view, c.1900 · Zeno.org · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Anna Pávlovna's salon
pins.story: Where it opens: a glittering, chilly salon in July 1805, the talk all of Napoleon and Bonaparte, into which the awkward, illegitimate Pierre blunders and from which the bored Prince Andrei is escaping to the war.

image.caption: The Palace Embankment on the Neva, 1820s - the elegant, chilly capital of the opening salons.
image.credit: Karl Beggrov, 1820s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Rostóvs' house, and the fire
pins.story: The Rostóvs' Moscow: Natasha's name-day, the balls, the whole loud life of the family - and then, in 1812, the city given up to Napoleon and set alight, where Pierre stays behind and is taken prisoner.

image.caption: Red Square, Moscow, 1801 - St Basil's and the Kremlin wall eleven years before the fire; the living city the invasion destroys.
image.credit: Fyodor Alekseyev, 1801 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bald Hills
pins.story: The severe country seat of old Prince Bolkónsky, where Andrei leaves his pregnant wife for the war - and which, in 1812, lies directly in the path of the advancing French: the old prince dies of a stroke as they approach, and Princess Marya is nearly trapped by her own mutinous peasants.

image.caption: A faded neoclassical manor behind an overgrown garden - an indicative view of the Bolkónsky seat at Bald Hills.
image.credit: Vasily Polenov, ‘Grandmother's Garden’, 1878 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Otrádnoe
pins.story: The pastoral heart of the book: the wolf hunt, Christmas mummers and the moonlit sleigh-ride - and the summer night when Andrei, passing through, overhears Natasha at her window and feels life return to him.

image.caption: A troika through the winter snow - the vitality of the Rostóvs' country idyll at Otrádnoe.
image.credit: Nikolai Sverchkov, 19th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the field of Austerlitz
pins.story: The climax of the 1805 war and Andrei's first disillusion: carrying the flag forward, he is struck down, and lies looking up at the infinite sky, all his dreams of glory suddenly small.

image.caption: The Battle of Austerlitz, 2 December 1805 - Napoleon and his marshals amid the captured standards and the fallen.
image.credit: François Gérard, 1810 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Tilsit
pins.story: The peace of the two Emperors, embracing on a raft on the Niemen - which Nikolái, among the army, watches in bewilderment, unable to square the friendship of emperors with the friends he has seen die.

image.caption: The meeting of Napoleon and Alexander on the raft at Tilsit, 1807 - the monogrammed pavilion of the quote.
image.credit: Adolphe Roehn, 1808 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Niemen
pins.story: Where the invasion is born and where it dies. In June 1812 four hundred thousand men pour over three bridges into Russia while the Emperor watches through a spyglass; in December a frozen fraction of them recross the same river.

image.caption: The Grande Armée crossing the Niemen into Russia, June 1812 - the columns pouring over the bridges, the cavalry fording the river.
image.credit: J. A. Clark, aquatint, 1816 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Vílna
pins.story: The advance's first halt and the retreat's last: Napoleon pauses here in June, master of the road to Moscow; six months later the wreck of his army drags itself back through the same streets.

pins.name: Smolénsk
pins.story: The French take Smolénsk by fire in August, and the road to Moscow - and to the Bolkónsky estate just past it - lies open. Later the starving retreat drags back through its ruins; and it is near here that Pierre, a prisoner, is at last set free.

image.caption: The burning of Smolénsk, August 1812 - the city ablaze across the valley, the people fleeing before the advance.
image.credit: Peter von Hess, 1846 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the field of Borodinó
pins.story: The book's centre: Pierre, a civilian in a white hat, wanders the field and ends amid the carnage of the Raévski redoubt, understanding nothing and everything; Andrei's regiment stands for hours under fire, and he is mortally wounded by a shell.

image.caption: Napoleon on the heights of Borodinó, 7 September 1812 - the Emperor slumped among his marshals, over a battle no one commands.
image.credit: Vasily Vereshchagin, 1897 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Yaroslávl
pins.story: Where the flight from Moscow comes to rest and the deathbed is set: Natasha, and then Marya, nurse the dying Andrei through his last days, until he reaches a strange serene acceptance and is gone.

pins.name: Málo-Yaroslávets
pins.story: The hinge of the retreat: barred from a fresh road south, the French are forced back onto their own devastated track, and the long starving, freezing death-march begins in earnest.

image.caption: The Battle of Málo-Yaroslávets, October 1812 - the town that turned the retreat back onto its own ravaged road.
image.credit: Peter von Hess, mid-19th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Berëzina
pins.story: The catastrophe of the retreat: at the broken bridges over the half-frozen river the whole misery of the army is concentrated into one moment, and thousands drown or are cut down in the crush.

image.caption: The retreat: Napoleon and his marshals in the frozen birch woods - the ruined army crawling west toward the Berëzina.
image.credit: Vasily Vereshchagin, ‘On the High Road’, 1887–1895 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Moscow
pins.story: Where the book opens, in confusion: Stiva Oblonsky has been caught in an affair, and his sister Anna comes from Petersburg to make peace. Levin comes to propose to Kitty and is refused; at the ball, Vronsky abandons Kitty for Anna, and everything begins.

image.caption: The Kremlin above the Moskva river - Moscow, where Kitty refuses Levin and Anna first dances with Vronsky.
image.credit: Photochrom, c.1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Saint Petersburg
pins.story: The Karenins' world, and society's tribunal. Here Anna lives with her husband and her son; here the affair becomes a scandal, Karenin refuses her a divorce, and the drawing-rooms shut their doors.

image.caption: Nevsky Prospekt towards the Admiralty spire - Petersburg, the Karenins' city and society's cold court on Anna's fall.
image.credit: Photochrom, 1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bologoye
pins.story: The hinge of the affair. Fleeing Moscow by the night train, Anna steps onto a blizzard-swept platform for air - and Vronsky is there, having followed her, to say that wherever she is, is where he must be.

image.caption: Bologoye station on the Petersburg–Moscow line, where Anna steps out into the snowstorm and finds Vronsky has followed her.
image.credit: Postcard, 1870s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Krasnoe Selo
pins.story: The officers' steeplechase. Vronsky rides his mare Frou-Frou to the front, then breaks her back with a clumsy movement in the saddle - and Anna, watching, cries out so nakedly that her husband beside her knows the truth.

pins.name: Pokrovskoye
pins.story: Levin's ground, and the book's answer to Anna's ruin. He mows with the peasants, wrestles with faith and farming, brings Kitty home as his bride, sees his son born, and finds, at the last, a reason to live.

image.caption: Mowing time - Levin's estate is unlocated Tula country; this is the haymaking he takes up alongside his peasants. Grigory Myasoyedov, 'Mowing Time', 1887.
image.credit: Grigory Myasoyedov, 1887 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Vozdvizhenskoye
pins.story: Vronsky and Anna's grand modern establishment - the model hospital, the English ways, the wealth. Dolly visits and sees, under the polish, how little happiness it holds, and how the affair has begun to crack.

pins.name: Kashin
pins.story: The provincial nobility elections. Levin comes hoping to believe in public duty and leaves disillusioned; Vronsky is there too, and the two men who love the same women circle each other in the crowd.

image.caption: Kashin, the provincial town where the nobility gather for the elections that draw Levin and Vronsky into the same hall - photographed by Prokudin-Gorsky, c.1910.
image.credit: Sergei Prokudin-Gorsky, c.1910 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bad Soden am Taunus
pins.story: Kitty's convalescence after Vronsky's humiliation. Among the invalids and the pious she meets Varenka and tries, and fails, at saintly self-denial - and slowly gets well enough to go home and, at last, to Levin.

image.caption: The drinking-hall in the spa park at Bad Soden am Taunus, where Kitty takes the waters to mend - this view a little later, c.1920.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1920 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: an unnamed town in Italy
pins.story: The brief idyll abroad. Free of Russia, Anna and Vronsky are happy for a while; he plays at painting until the real painter Mihailov shames the dabbling - and paints a portrait of Anna truer than Vronsky's own. Then the boredom starts.

image.caption: Tolstoy leaves the town unnamed; Anna and Vronsky's Italian months are placed at Venice - the Grand Canal, photochrom, c.1890s.
image.credit: Photochrom, c.1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Obiralovka
pins.story: The end. Half-mad with jealousy, Anna takes a train out to intercept Vronsky and, on the platform, throws herself beneath a goods wagon - the crushed watchman of the first chapter come round at last for her.

image.caption: A station on a Russian line - Obiralovka itself went unphotographed; this platform (Borodino, c.1910) stands for the suburban stop where Anna steps under the train.
image.credit: Sergei Prokudin-Gorsky, c.1910 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Serbia
pins.story: Vronsky's exit. Broken by Anna's death and courting his own, he boards a volunteer train for the Serbian front, seeing his squadron off toward a war Tolstoy plainly doubts. Koznyshev watches him go - the last we see of him.

image.caption: Serbian troops with a field gun, 1876 - the war Vronsky rides south to join once Anna is dead.
image.credit: Photograph, 1876 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: St. Petersburg
pins.story: Huck's home, and the trap he flees. Newly rich from treasure and half-heartedly 'sivilized' by the Widow Douglas, he is stalked by his drunken Pap - who wants the money, and locks the boy up. Huck fakes his own murder to get free.

image.caption: Hannibal, Missouri, from across the river, c.1904 - the real town behind Twain's St Petersburg.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., 1904 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Jackson's Island
pins.story: The hiding place, and the meeting. Here Huck lies low - and finds Jim, who has run from Miss Watson rather than be sold away from his family. The two make the island home until the hunt closes in, then take to a raft.

image.caption: A Mississippi lumber raft riding the current between wooded shores - the kind of raft Huck and Jim build and slip away on. From Twain's own 'Life on the Mississippi', 1883.
image.credit: E. H. Garrett, 1883 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: St. Louis
pins.story: The big city, passed by night - a blaze of lights on the black water, and gone. The raft keeps to the current, hiding by day and floating by night, bound for Cairo.

image.caption: The levee at St Louis, 1890 - wagons, cordwood and moored sternwheelers, the Eads Bridge beyond. The city the raft drifts past in the dark.
image.credit: Robert Benecke, 1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Cairo
pins.story: The turning point they never reach. Here the Ohio joins the Mississippi, and here Huck and Jim mean to sell the raft and steam up the Ohio to the free States. In a thick fog they slip past Cairo unseeing - and Jim's road to freedom goes with it.

pins.name: the Grangerfords'
pins.story: The aristocratic house where Huck is taken in after a steamboat runs down the raft. Kind, cultured people locked in a murderous feud with the Shepherdsons - which ends before Huck's eyes in a riverbank killing. He flees back to the mended raft and to Jim.

image.caption: A lower-Mississippi sternwheeler under way, c.1900 - a boat like the one that smashes through the raft in the dark near here, and parts Huck from Jim.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bricksville
pins.story: A shabby Arkansas town where the two con men who have seized the raft - a self-styled duke and king - fleece the crowd with a crooked show. In the same dusty street Huck watches a harmless drunk shot dead for his taunts.

image.caption: The working riverfront of a lower-Mississippi levee town, c.1900 (Vicksburg) - the kind of shabby river town where the frauds put on their show.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co., c.1900 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the town of the Wilks brothers
pins.story: Here the frauds try their cruellest swindle: posing as the English brothers of a dead man to rob his orphaned nieces of their inheritance. Sickened, Huck turns against the pair, helps expose them, and barely escapes back to the raft.

image.caption: Wooded bluffs above the lower Mississippi - the wild river country the raft slips through between the towns. Joseph Rusling Meeker, 1881.
image.credit: Joseph Rusling Meeker, 1881 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Silas Phelps's place, near Pikesville
pins.story: The end of the road. The king sells Jim here for forty dollars; Huck, alone, decides to damn himself rather than betray his friend - and, by chance, walks into the farm of Tom Sawyer's own relatives, who take him for Tom.

pins.name: the Reform Club
pins.story: Where the wager is struck over whist and the Daily Telegraph's timetable, and where - eighty days later - Fogg must be back by a quarter before nine to win. He leaves at twenty-five past seven that same evening.

image.caption: The Reform Club, Pall Mall, wood engraving, 1896
image.credit: Wood engraving, 1896 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the station
pins.story: The party takes the evening train for the Continent, bound over the Channel for Paris and the Mont Cenis road to Brindisi. The race has begun.

pins.name: Paris
pins.story: A crossing of the city between stations on the run south to the Alps; Passepartout barely glimpses it.

image.caption: The Boulevard Montmartre, Paris, c.1870
image.credit: Art Institute of Chicago · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Turin
pins.story: Reached by the Mont Cenis tunnel and pass on the morning of Friday 4 October - the Alps crossed, Italy ahead.

image.caption: Turin, the Piazza Castello, c.1880
image.credit: Hallwyl Museum, c.1880 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Mont Cenis
pins.story: The Alpine crossing between France and Italy named in the Telegraph's itinerary; the Fréjus (Mont Cenis) railway tunnel had opened only the year before, in 1871.

image.caption: The Mont Cenis (Fell) mountain railway at Grand Croix, 1871
image.credit: Giacomo Brogi, 1871 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Brindisi
pins.story: The Adriatic railhead where the P&O steamer Mongolia takes on the Indian mails. Fogg boards on the Saturday afternoon and sails at five.

image.caption: Brindisi railway station, the P&O railhead, c.1870
image.credit: c.1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Suez
pins.story: At the head of the Red Sea, where Fix waits with a warrant that never comes and inspects the Mongolia's passengers. Passepartout has his passport stamped; the detective attaches himself to the party here.

image.caption: The opening of the Suez Canal at Ismailia, 1869
image.credit: Stereoview, 1869 · Rijksmuseum · CC0

pins.name: Aden
pins.story: The Mongolia's coaling stop at the mouth of the Red Sea. Fogg and his servant go ashore for four hours to have the passport stamped again; the ship makes up her lost time and steams on for Bombay.

image.caption: Aden, Steamer Point, 1855–56
image.credit: Auguste Bartholdi, 1855–56 · Bibliothèque nationale de France · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Bombay
pins.story: India reached ahead of time. Passepartout wanders into a pagoda in his shoes and is set upon; the Great Indian Peninsula Railway carries them east that same evening - and that small blasphemy will hand Fix his pretext later.

image.caption: Bombay harbour, photographed c.1870
image.credit: Albumen photograph, c.1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the hamlet of Kholby
