pins.story: Here the railway simply stops: fifty miles of track never laid, though the tickets are sold through. Fogg buys an elephant, Kiouni, at a fabulous price, hires a guide, and sets off across the forest for Allahabad.

image.caption: A bullock cart on a forest track in India - an indicative view of the central-Indian jungle the railway crossed, c.1900
image.credit: c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the pagoda of Pillaji
pins.story: Deep in the forest the party comes on a suttee: the young widow Aouda drugged with opium and hemp, to be burned at dawn beside her dead rajah. At the last moment Passepartout, hidden on the pyre, rises as the dead prince and carries her off.

image.caption: The Great Pagoda at Tanjore, 1798 - an indicative view of a South Indian Hindu pagoda
image.credit: Thomas & William Daniell, 1798 · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Allahabad, the City of God
pins.story: Where the rails begin again. The elephant is given to the guide as reward; the party takes the train down the Ganges valley, buying European clothes for Aouda before boarding.

image.caption: The Ganges ghat below Allahabad Fort, photographed c.1858–61
image.credit: Metropolitan Museum of Art, c.1858–61 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Benares
pins.story: Sir Francis Cromarty's destination, where the brigadier leaves the party. The train presses on down the Ganges for Calcutta.

image.caption: The burning ghats and Aurangzeb’s mosque on the Ganges at Benares, c.1860
image.credit: Rijksmuseum, c.1850–1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Calcutta
pins.story: The party is haled before a magistrate over the Bombay pagoda - Fix's doing - and Fogg buys their way free with bail forfeited. They reach the steamer Rangoon with minutes to spare, bound for Hong Kong by way of Singapore.

image.caption: Shipping on the Hooghly river at Calcutta, c.1860
image.credit: Francis Frith, c.1850–1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Singapore
pins.story: A coaling call on the Rangoon's run to Hong Kong. A few hours ashore among nutmeg groves and clove plantations, then out past the mountains of Malacca.

image.caption: Singapore harbour, c.1880
image.credit: Rijksmuseum, c.1870–1900 · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

pins.name: Hong Kong
pins.story: The last British ground before Japan - where Fix's warrant might at last be valid, and is not. He gets Passepartout drunk and drugged in an opium den so he cannot warn Fogg; the Carnatic sails a day early, carrying the servant off senseless and leaving master and detective behind. Fogg hires the pilot-boat Tankadere to chase her.

image.caption: Victoria Harbour, Hong Kong, from the Botanical Gardens, c.1870
image.credit: William Pryor Floyd, c.1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Shanghai
pins.story: The Tankadere's true goal: not Yokohama itself but Shanghai, where the American mail steamer for San Francisco calls. Through a gale the little schooner catches the General Grant off the coast and signals her down.

image.caption: The Bund, Shanghai, c.1869
image.credit: John Thomson, c.1869 · Metropolitan Museum of Art · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

pins.name: Yokohama
pins.story: Passepartout wakes here aboard the Carnatic, penniless and alone, and joins Batulcar's acrobatic troupe of Long Noses to earn his passage. Fogg arrives on the General Grant and finds his servant at the bottom of a human pyramid; the party, reunited, sails for America.

image.caption: Yokohama, the treaty port, c.1865–70
image.credit: Rijksmuseum, c.1860–80 · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

pins.name: San Francisco
pins.story: America, reached on 3 December after twenty-two days of Pacific. A political brawl in the street, a first sight of the New World - and that evening the party boards the Pacific Railroad train east.

image.caption: San Francisco from Nob Hill - from Muybridge’s panorama, 1878
image.credit: Eadweard Muybridge, 1878 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Ogden
pins.story: Where the Central Pacific hands the line to the Union Pacific, and the party breaks the journey to run up the branch to Salt Lake City for a glimpse of the Mormon capital.

image.caption: Ogden, Utah, on the Pacific Railroad, c.1870
image.credit: C. R. Savage, c.1870 · Brigham Young University · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Great Salt Lake
pins.story: The railroad rounds the great inland sea of the Mormons. A fellow passenger delivers Passepartout a whole lecture on Mormon history at twenty miles an hour.

image.caption: A railroad trestle in the Great Salt Lake valley at Promontory, 1868
image.credit: A. J. Russell, 1868 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Medicine Bow
pins.story: A failing suspension bridge over a torrent. The engineer takes it at full speed to jump the gap; the train clears it and the bridge falls into the rapids behind.

image.caption: A. J. Russell's photograph of the Union Pacific's high timber trestle at Dale Creek, Wyoming, c.1868 - indicative of the shaky bridge Fogg's train clears at full speed near Medicine Bow.
image.credit: Andrew J. Russell · Beinecke Library, Yale · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Plum Creek
pins.story: On the plains a war-party of Sioux storms the train. In the running fight Passepartout uncouples the locomotive to stop the cars at the fort - and is carried off by the Indians.

image.caption: A Union Pacific station on the Nebraska plains in 1869, freight cars at the platform - indicative of the small prairie stops like Plum Creek, where a Sioux war-party storms Fogg's train.
image.credit: U.S. Geological Survey, 1869 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Fort Kearney
pins.story: The train halts at the fort for protection. Fogg gives up the wager without hesitation to lead a party of soldiers after his servant - and rescues him. But the train has gone on without them; they are stranded, and must find another way to Omaha.

image.caption: The stage station at Fort Kearny on the Platte, drawn in 1863 with its coaches for Omaha - the post where Fogg leaves the wager to rescue Passepartout, and is stranded, needing another way east.
image.credit: 1863 lithograph · U.S. National Archives · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Omaha
pins.story: The eastern terminus of the Pacific Railroad proper, reached by wind-sledge across the frozen prairie after the train was lost. From here fast trains run through Chicago to the Atlantic seaboard.

image.caption: Omaha, Nebraska, eastern terminus of the Pacific Railroad, c.1868
image.credit: Jackson Brothers, c.1865–70 · New York Public Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Chicago
pins.story: Reached on the 10th, 'already risen from its ruins' a year after the Great Fire. Fogg changes trains at once for the run east to New York.

image.caption: Chicago from Lake Michigan in an 1874 Currier & Ives bird's-eye view - the city already risen from its ruins, where Fogg changes at once for the run east to New York.
image.credit: Currier & Ives, 1874 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: New York
pins.story: They reach the quay forty-five minutes after the Liverpool steamer China has sailed. Fogg charters the trading vessel Henrietta instead, buys off her crew, and - when her captain refuses Liverpool - takes command himself.

image.caption: New York harbour and the East River, Currier & Ives, 1879
image.credit: C.R. Parsons for Currier & Ives, 1879 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Queenstown
pins.story: The Henrietta, her upperworks burned for fuel, raises the Irish coast and Fastnet Light. Rather than crawl the last miles to Liverpool on failing steam, Fogg lands here to take the mail express - the trick that gains twelve hours on the Atlantic steamers.

image.caption: Queenstown (Cobh) harbour, Co. Cork, c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Dublin
pins.story: The mail train runs them up from Queenstown by dawn, and they embark at once on the fast Kingstown packet across the Irish Sea for Liverpool.

image.caption: Sackville Street and O’Connell Bridge, Dublin, c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Liverpool quay
pins.story: Fogg steps ashore at twenty minutes to noon on the 21st, six hours from London and seemingly the winner - and there Fix, warrant in hand at last, arrests him. The lost hours in the Custom House will appear to cost him everything.

image.caption: Liverpool dockside on the Mersey, c.1897
image.credit: Liverpool Record Office, c.1897 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: London
pins.story: Freed once Fix realises his blunder, Fogg races for London believing himself five minutes too late. But travelling always eastward he has gained a full day at the meridian: it is not Sunday but Saturday, and he reaches the Reform Club with seconds to spare.

image.caption: The Houses of Parliament and the Thames, c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: my house at Maybury
pins.story: The narrator's home on the hill above Woking, where the book opens on an ordinary June and where, four weeks later, he comes home to find his wife alive after all. The exact house is Wells's invention; Maybury Hill is real.

image.caption: Chobham Road, Woking, in a postcard of 1903 - the town below Maybury Hill, where the narrator's invented house stands.
image.credit: Postcard, 1903 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the common
pins.story: The sand-pit on Horsell Common where the first cylinder falls and unscrews before a gawping crowd - and where the heat-ray first sweeps out. Ground zero of the invasion, and a real common you can still walk.

pins.name: Addlestone Golf Links
pins.story: Where the second cylinder comes down, on the golf links - one corner of the triangle of pits from which the Martians first spread.

pins.name: Pyrford
pins.story: The third cylinder's fall, closing the triangle of pits around Woking from which the fighting-machines advance.

pins.name: Leatherhead
pins.story: Where the narrator drives his wife to the safety of her cousins, twelve miles off - the last calm errand before the county comes apart. He turns straight round and heads back into it.

pins.name: Weybridge
pins.story: The Thames-side towns where the narrator watches the army make its stand - and sees a fighting-machine brought down for the first time, felled into the river as it wrecks Shepperton church across the water.

image.caption: The Thames at Weybridge, painted by Turner around 1805 - the river the narrator watches as the artillery duel opens across the water.
image.credit: J. M. W. Turner, The Thames at Weybridge (c.1805) · Tate · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the ruined house
pins.story: The white house where the narrator and the curate are trapped for a fortnight when the fifth cylinder falls almost on top of them - buried, half-starved, watching the Martians at their pit through a crack in the wall until the curate's nerve breaks. The house is Wells's invention; Sheen is real.

pins.name: Wimbledon
pins.story: Where the sixth star falls, and one of the hills from which the Martians press their crescent on London. The narrator's train home, at the very end, runs past its abandoned pit.

image.caption: Wimbledon Common and its windmill in an Edwardian postcard - the open ground the narrator crosses on the long walk east.
image.credit: Raphael Tuck & Sons 'Oilette' postcard, c.1905 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Putney Hill
pins.story: Climbing out of the wrecked south-west, the narrator meets the artilleryman again - full of grand, idle schemes for a resistance in the drains while he digs a hole and plays cards. The narrator leaves him to it and walks on into the silence.

pins.name: Primrose Hill
pins.story: The Martians' final redoubt, where the seventh cylinder fell - and where the narrator, walking a dead and silent London, climbs to find the fighting-machines fallen still and their masters dead in the pit, killed by the humblest bacteria of the earth they came to take.

pins.name: near Regent's Park
pins.story: The brother's lodgings, where his separate story begins: a medical student who reads of Woking on the Saturday and by Monday is running for his life. Wells names the district, not the address.

image.caption: Albert Terrace, on the edge of Regent's Park, in 1905 - near where the narrator's brother joins the panic streaming north out of London.
image.credit: Charles Martin postcard, 1905 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Barnet
pins.story: On the road north out of London the brother is swept into the full exodus - the choked lanes, the crush at the crossroads, the whole population of the city pouring away. Here he throws in with two women in a pony-chaise and defends them from the mob.

image.caption: High Barnet seen from Barnet Hill, c.1900 - the road north out of London down which the refugee crowds pour.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Chelmsford
pins.story: Where a self-appointed 'Committee of Public Supply' seizes the party's pony for food, and the road to the coast has to be finished on foot.

image.caption: High Street, Chelmsford, in 1906 - an Essex market town on the refugees' road to the coast.
image.credit: Fred Spalding postcard, 1906 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Tillingham
pins.story: The little Essex village near the coast the brother's party passes at midday, within sight at last of the sea and the crowd of ships waiting to carry the refugees away.

pins.name: the Blackwater
pins.story: The Essex estuary where the brother gets aboard a crowded paddle-steamer - the sea black with boats, everyone bound for the Continent, when three fighting-machines come wading out into the water after them.

image.caption: A crowded pleasure steamer leaving Maldon on the Blackwater, 1906 - indicative of the Essex estuary where the fleeing boats gather off the coast.
image.credit: Postcard, 1906 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the open sea
pins.story: The open water off the Blackwater's mouth where the ironclad ram Thunder Child charges the Martians to buy the steamers their escape - and goes down doing it, the one clear victory in the whole rout.

image.caption: The torpedo-ram HMS Polyphemus at sea - the Royal Navy's one true ram, of the very kind the Thunder Child was, that charges the Martians off the Essex coast.
image.credit: Royal Navy photograph, c.1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Westminster
pins.story: Clarissa sets out to buy the flowers, returns to prepare, receives Peter's surprise visit, and holds her party here.

image.caption: Westminster Abbey from the Dean's Yard side, the precinct where the Dalloways' house is placed, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Bond Street
pins.story: Clarissa walks up Bond Street for her flowers; here the morning's first convergence gathers, as a car backfires and the whole street - Clarissa, Septimus, Rezia - looks the same way at once.

image.caption: Bond Street, Mayfair, etched by Ian Strang in 1929 - the shopping street Clarissa walks up for her flowers, where the backfiring motor car halts the whole pavement.
image.credit: Ian Strang etching, 1929 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Mulberry's the florists
pins.story: Clarissa buys her flowers from Miss Pym when a car backfires outside - the morning's first great convergence, and the moment she and Septimus stand unknowing in the same crowd.

pins.name: Hatchards' shop window
pins.story: Clarissa stops at the bookshop window and reads the open page of Cymbeline - 'Fear no more the heat o' the sun' - the day's recurring refrain.

image.caption: Piccadilly, the street of Hatchards' bookshop, on a postcard of about 1910 - Clarissa pauses at the shop window over an open Cymbeline, 'Fear no more the heat o' the sun'.
image.credit: LL postcard, c.1910 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Regent's Park in the Broad Walk
pins.story: Septimus and Rezia sit on a bench while the aeroplane skywrites overhead and he sees the dead Evans; here, at the fountain, Rezia cries 'I am alone', and here Peter Walsh, dozing nearby, passes the same couple without knowing them.

image.caption: Regent's Park at Albert Terrace, on a postcard of 1905 - the park where Septimus and Rezia wait out the hours and he hears the dead Evans singing. The Broad Walk of the scene itself was never photographed for the public record; this is the park's terraced edge.
image.credit: Charles Martin postcard, 1905 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Harley Street
pins.story: Septimus and Rezia walk down Harley Street to their noon appointment with Sir William Bradshaw, who condemns Septimus to a rest-home.

pins.name: the large Bloomsbury-lodging house window
pins.story: The Smiths' rented rooms; cornered by Dr Holmes, Septimus throws himself from the window that afternoon.

image.caption: The corner of Tottenham Court Road, thronged with horse omnibuses about 1901 - the Warren Smiths lodge just off this road, and from its window Septimus throws himself.
image.credit: LL postcard, 1901 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Brook Street
pins.story: Lady Bruton's Mayfair luncheon, which pointedly excludes Clarissa; Richard Dalloway and the smug courtier Hugh Whitbread lunch here and help draft her letter on emigration to Canada.

pins.name: the Green Park
pins.story: Richard Dalloway, carrying red and white roses home to tell Clarissa he loves her, crosses Green Park past the sprawling poor and a female vagrant.

pins.name: the gates of Buckingham Palace
pins.story: The presumed destination of the grey motor car; a crowd waits at the Palace gates and along the Mall, and the skywriting aeroplane roars over them.

image.caption: Buckingham Palace and the Victoria Memorial from the Mall, photographed c.1912
image.credit: Judges Ltd. postcard, c.1912 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Whitehall
pins.story: Peter Walsh marches up Whitehall after leaving Clarissa, overtaken by a column of uniformed boys bound for the empty tomb; later Elizabeth's omnibus rushes up the same street.

image.caption: Whitehall, the Horse Guards, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Trafalgar Square
pins.story: Peter Walsh, alone and unknown at half-past eleven, feels a sudden exhilaration under Gordon's statue, then follows a young woman across the square towards the Haymarket.

image.caption: Trafalgar Square, Nelson's Column and the fountains, photographed c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: the Army and Navy Stores
pins.story: Miss Kilman takes Elizabeth to tea and clings to her miserably; Elizabeth escapes into Victoria Street to catch a bus.

pins.name: the Strand
pins.story: Elizabeth rides the omnibus up Whitehall and the Strand - a rare venture east - past Somerset House, dreaming of a profession, before turning for home.

image.caption: The Strand, looking east, photographed 1901
image.credit: H. C. White Co. stereograph, 1901 · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Bloomsbury
pins.story: Peter Walsh's hotel among the lit squares; here he dines, and from here he walks to the party opening the big blade of his pocket-knife.

image.caption: The British Museum, at the heart of Bloomsbury, in a photochrom of the 1890s - the district of lit squares where Peter Walsh keeps his hotel and dines alone before the party.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890s · Library of Congress · public domain

pins.name: Greenwich
pins.story: The skywriting aeroplane is followed east out of the West End until it is lost over Greenwich, where Mr Bentley, rolling his lawn, watches it dwindle to a symbol of the soul.

image.caption: The prospect from One Tree Hill in Greenwich Park over the Royal Naval College, the Thames and its masts, and London beyond - engraving after Pieter Tillemans, c.1752
image.credit: Pieter Tillemans / Robert Sayer engraving, c.1752 · National Maritime Museum, Greenwich · public domain

pins.name: Gatsby’s mansion
pins.realName: the Gold Coast shore of Great Neck, Long Island
pins.story: The huge imitation-Norman mansion where Jay Gatsby throws his blazing summer parties for hundreds who don’t know him - all of it a lure, a lighthouse lit for one guest across the bay: Daisy.

pins.name: Nick’s cottage
pins.story: The narrator Nick Carraway’s cheap bungalow, wedged beside Gatsby’s palace. Here Gatsby and Daisy are brought together again after five years - a reunion Nick arranges, over tea, in the rain.

pins.name: the Buchanans’ house
pins.realName: Land’s End, Sands Point, Long Island
pins.story: The Buchanans’ cool, moneyed house on the fashionable side of the bay - old wealth, careless and secure. Here Nick first dines with his cousin Daisy and her rich, brutal, unfaithful husband Tom.

pins.name: the green light
pins.realName: the end of Daisy’s dock, East Egg
pins.story: The green light at the end of Daisy’s dock, which Gatsby stretches his arms toward across the dark water - the whole of his hope reduced to one far, faint point, a few hundred yards and five years away.

pins.name: the valley of ashes
pins.story: The grey dumping-ground everyone must drive through between the money and the city, watched over by the blind billboard eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg. Here George Wilson keeps his garage, his wife Myrtle is Tom’s mistress - and here, on the road, Myrtle is run down and killed.

pins.name: the Plaza Hotel
pins.story: A stifling suite where it all comes apart: Tom, cornered, exposes Gatsby as a bootlegger and a fraud, and makes Daisy choose. She cannot leave her husband for the dream, and Gatsby’s whole edifice cracks in the heat.

image.caption: The Plaza Hotel and Grand Army Plaza at the corner of Central Park, New York, photographed c.1907.
image.credit: New York Public Library (via Wikimedia Commons) · NYPL Digital Gallery b19806373 · public domain

pins.name: the flat on 158th Street
pins.story: The little flat Tom keeps for his mistress Myrtle Wilson, where a drunken afternoon party swells and turns ugly - Tom, when Myrtle taunts him with Daisy’s name, breaking her nose with the flat of his hand.

pins.name: Wolfsheim’s restaurant
pins.story: The basement lunch-room where Gatsby introduces Nick to Meyer Wolfsheim, the gambler who fixed the World Series - a glimpse of the criminal money that built the mansion and paid for the parties.

pins.name: Louisville
pins.story: Where it began, five years before: a poor young officer at nearby Camp Taylor falls for the golden Daisy Fay, then is sent to the war. By the time he returns she has married rich Tom Buchanan - and Gatsby sets out to become worthy of her.

image.caption: Fourth Avenue, Louisville, Kentucky - Daisy’s home town - photographed c.1906.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. · Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division (LC 2016806016) · public domain

pins.name: the Middle West
pins.realName: a city in the Middle West (St Paul, Minnesota)
pins.story: The solid, dull Middle West Nick comes east to escape - and, sickened by the careless glamour that leaves Gatsby dead and the Buchanans untouched, the place he finally retreats to, wanting the world “at a moral attention forever”.

image.caption: St. Paul, Minnesota, seen across the Mississippi c.1902 - the Middle-Western city Fitzgerald drew Nick’s unnamed home from.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. · Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division (LC 2016802215) · public domain

pins.name: North Dakota
pins.story: Where Jay Gatsby began as plain James Gatz, a poor farm boy who never accepted his parents’ lot - and who, somewhere on the road out, invented a grander self and set out to become him.

image.caption: Broadway, Fargo, North Dakota, in 1909 - the state James Gatz came from, its largest town standing in for the whole.
image.credit: Photographic postcard, W. O. Olsen, 1909 · Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division (LC 2024691666) · public domain

pins.name: Lake Superior
pins.realName: the south shore of Lake Superior
pins.story: On the lake, the drifting teenager rows out to a millionaire’s yacht, warns him of a storm, and is taken up by the old copper-and-silver man Dan Cody. Five years cruising the continent make him, at last, into Gatsby.

image.caption: The harbour and grain elevators at Duluth on Lake Superior, a lake steamer at the docks, c.1905 - the lake where Gatz met Dan Cody’s yacht, not the exact spot.
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. · Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division (LC 2016801062) · public domain

pins.name: Raskolnikov’s garret
pins.story: The “coffin” of a garret where the poor ex-student Raskolnikov lies for days, starving and feverish, and hatches his theory that an extraordinary man may step over any law - even to murder. Everything in the book begins and ends within a few hundred yards of this door.

pins.name: the pawnbroker’s flat
pins.story: The flat of the old money-lender Alyona Ivanovna. Here Raskolnikov brings the axe down on her, and then, when her gentle sister Lizaveta walks in, on her too - the second, unplanned killing that damns him. The crime the whole book is the punishment for.

image.caption: The Ekaterininsky (now Griboyedov) Canal and its embankment houses, photographed c.1905-1915 - the canal and its milieu, not the pawnbroker’s exact house (the church at left post-dates the novel).
image.credit: Sergey Prokudin-Gorsky · Library of Congress via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Sonia’s room
pins.story: Where Sonia Marmeladov - driven onto the streets to feed her stepmother’s children - reads Raskolnikov the raising of Lazarus, and where he first confesses the murder to her. She becomes the one soul he cannot lie to, and the one who will follow him to Siberia.

pins.name: the Hay Market
pins.realName: Sennaya Ploshchad (the Hay Market), St Petersburg
pins.story: The teeming square Raskolnikov is compulsively drawn back to, day after day, as if the crime were a magnet. Here, near the end, on Sonia’s counsel, he kneels down and kisses the filthy earth before the whole market - the first act of his confession.

image.caption: Sennaya Ploshchad (the Hay Market), St Petersburg, with its church, hay carts and market crowds; lithograph after Alexander Brullov, 1822 - the same square Raskolnikov haunts.
image.credit: Alexander Brullov · Web Gallery of Art via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the police bureau
pins.realName: the district police bureau (Sadovaya 58, one of two candidates)
pins.story: Where Raskolnikov is first summoned over a trivial debt - and nearly faints when the talk turns to the murder - and where, at the very last, he climbs the stairs to say the words aloud: that it was he who killed the old pawnbroker and her sister.

pins.name: Porfiry’s lodging
pins.realName: Porfiry Petrovitch’s government flat, near the Hay Market
pins.story: Where the examining magistrate Porfiry Petrovitch draws Raskolnikov into two long, smiling interrogations - circling his own published theory that an “extraordinary man” has the right to step over blood - never accusing, always knowing. The mind that hunts the murderer as the murderer hunts himself.

pins.name: the tavern
pins.realName: a cellar pot-house near the Hay Market
pins.story: The reeking cellar where the ruined drunkard Marmeladov pours out his confession to Raskolnikov - his daughter Sonia driven onto the streets, his wife consumptive - and asks the question that haunts the book: do you understand what it means when you have nowhere left to turn?

pins.name: the Marmeladovs’ rooms
pins.realName: the Marmeladovs’ rooms (Kozel’s house), off the Hay Market
pins.story: The wretched room where Marmeladov, run down by a carriage in the street outside, is carried home to die in his family’s arms - and where, days later, the funeral dinner collapses into chaos as Luzhin frames Sonia for theft to spite Raskolnikov.

pins.name: the Crystal Palace tavern
pins.story: The tavern where Raskolnikov, half-daring discovery, reads the newspaper report of the murder and taunts the young police clerk Zametov - and one of the drinking-places where, later, he crosses paths with the cold, watchful Svidrigaïlov, his own dark double.

pins.name: the stone in the yard
pins.story: Reeling from the murders, Raskolnikov shoves the pawnbroker’s purse and trinkets under a stone in an empty yard and never spends a rouble of it - the plunder was never the point, and the theory is already eating him alive.

pins.name: Svidrigaïlov’s lodging
pins.story: Where Svidrigaïlov - Raskolnikov’s dark double, a sensualist trailing ruined lives, rumoured to have driven his own wife to her grave - lodges beside Sonia to listen through the wall, corners Dounia and is driven off at gunpoint, and spends his final sleepless night before walking out to die.

pins.name: the canal bridge
pins.realName: Voznesensky Bridge over the Griboyedov Canal
pins.story: The canal bridge where Raskolnikov, staring into the black water, half-means to drown himself - until a desperate woman throws herself in before his eyes, and he turns away, unable even to die.

pins.name: Nikolaevsky Bridge
pins.story: On the great bridge over the Neva a carriage nearly rides him down and an old woman in a kerchief, taking him for a beggar, presses a coin into his hand “in Christ’s name”. He stares at the cold shining panorama of the capital that has no place for him, and flings the coin into the river.

image.caption: The Nikolaevsky Bridge over the Neva (today’s Blagoveshchensky Bridge), with the chapel of St Nicholas and St Isaac’s beyond; photographed 1861-1865, the novel’s own decade.
image.credit: A. F. Lorens · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the watchtower
pins.story: At grey dawn Svidrigaïlov walks to the fire-tower on the city’s edge, tells the bewildered sentry he is “going to America”, and shoots himself - the double who takes the other road out, the one Raskolnikov turns away from.

image.caption: A St Petersburg police-and-fire station house with its watchtower (kalancha); lithograph after André Durand, 1839 - an indicative view of the kind of fire-tower before which Svidrigaïlov shoots himself, not the Syezzhinskaya tower itself.
image.credit: André Durand, 1839 lithograph · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the prison on the Irtish
pins.realName: the convict fortress at Omsk, on the Irtish
pins.story: Two thousand miles east, across the Urals and the whole width of Russia, Raskolnikov serves eight years’ penal servitude - and here, at last, with Sonia settled nearby and waiting, the ice in him begins to break, and a new life he has not yet earned or understood opens before him.

image.caption: The Tara Gate of the Omsk fortress - the surviving gate of the ostrog where Dostoevsky himself served katorga, the model for Raskolnikov’s Siberian prison; postcard photograph, 1916.
image.credit: Suvorin & Co. postcard, Scherer, Nabholz & Co., Moscow, 1916 · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Kellynch Hall
pins.realName: Barrington Court, near Crewkerne, Somerset (the scholar-favoured model)
pins.story: The Elliot family seat, and the vanity that beggars it: Sir Walter Elliot, too proud and too extravagant to live within his rank, must let the great house to strangers and retreat to cheaper Bath. Here too, eight years before, Anne loved and gave up Frederick Wentworth. The old landed order, quietly failing.

pins.name: Uppercross
pins.realName: Hinton St George, Somerset (the model); three miles from Kellynch
pins.story: The warm, noisy, unbookish Musgrove world where Anne is sent to be useful - to nurse her sister Mary’s complaints and watch Captain Wentworth court the two lively Musgrove girls, Henrietta and Louisa, as if Anne were already old and finished at seven-and-twenty.

pins.name: Winthrop
pins.story: The plain down-a-valley farm of the Hayter cousins, reached by the long autumn nutting-walk. In the hedgerow above it Anne overhears Wentworth praising Louisa’s firmness of mind - a speech that sounds, painfully, like a reproach of Anne’s own long-ago yielding.

pins.name: the Cobb
pins.story: The great curving sea-wall where the whole book turns. Headstrong Louisa Musgrove, in high spirits, insists on being “jumped down” the steep steps, misjudges it, and is taken up senseless on the pavement. In the panic it is quiet Anne who keeps her head - and Wentworth, watching, begins to understand what he threw away.

image.caption: Lyme Regis and its harbour, the Cobb and its shipping across the bay; W. B. Cooke’s 1814 line engraving after J. M. W. Turner - exactly Anne Elliot’s day.
image.credit: Library of Congress · Cooke after Turner, 1814 · LCCN 2005696464 · public domain

pins.name: the Harvilles’ lodgings
pins.story: The snug, thrifty seaside home of Captain Harville - a wounded officer making a warm life on little money, the navy’s domestic virtue set against the Elliots’ cold splendour. Here grieving Captain Benwick reads his poetry, and here Louisa recovers - and, in the end, quietly attaches herself to Benwick, setting Wentworth free.

image.caption: Lyme Regis from the shore in a squall, the town and its sea wall; Thomas Jeavons’s 1836 engraving after Turner.
image.credit: Yale Center for British Art · Jeavons after Turner, 1836 · B1977.14.13406 · public domain

pins.name: Plymouth
pins.story: The naval world the Crofts come from - Admiral Croft fresh off years afloat in the war just ended. That the sailors’ first home ashore is Kellynch Hall itself, rented from the ruined baronet, is the whole social argument of the book in a single move: the sea taking the land.

image.caption: Plymouth Sound crowded with the fleet, August 1815; John James Chalon’s 1816 painting - the naval world the Crofts came home from.
image.credit: National Maritime Museum, Greenwich · John James Chalon, 1816 · BHC3227 · public domain

pins.name: Camden Place
pins.story: The grand Bath address Sir Walter takes to keep up appearances he can no longer afford at home - perched high on the hill, all show and no comfort. Anne dreads it as an imprisonment; her father cares only that the rooms are large and the street fashionable.

image.caption: Bath seen from Camden Place, the terraces stepping down toward the Abbey; an 1841 engraving, a little later than Anne’s own day.
image.credit: Samborne, “The Poetic Guide to Clifton and Bristol” (1841) · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: the Pump Room
pins.story: The great rooms by the Abbey where all Bath is seen and sees - where the Elliots display their recovered consequence, where the smooth, calculating Mr Elliot presses his suit on Anne, and where, at a concert, Wentworth’s plain jealousy tells her at last that he loves her still.

image.caption: The Pump Room, Bath, with company taking the waters; a coloured aquatint published 1804, exactly Anne Elliot’s period.
image.credit: Rijksmuseum · aquatint “Bath: Pump-Room”, 1804 · RP-P-2015-26-1493 · public domain

pins.name: Milsom Street
pins.story: The busy shopping street where Anne, sheltering from a shower in Molland’s, comes suddenly face to face with Wentworth, arrived in Bath at last - and the long, tense, hopeful courtship-in-public begins, watched by all the wrong people.

image.caption: Milsom Street, Bath, the fashionable shopping street with promenaders and a carriage; Thomas Malton’s 1784 aquatint.
image.credit: National Gallery of Art (open access) · Thomas Malton the Younger, 1784 · NGA 76353 · public domain

pins.name: the White Hart
pins.realName: the White Hart inn, Stall Street, Bath
pins.story: The inn where, as Anne argues to Captain Harville that women love longest “when all hope is gone”, Wentworth sits writing at the next table - and pours it all into a letter, then lays it before her and flees. “You pierce my soul. I am half agony, half hope.”

image.caption: The White Hart inn, Bath, coaches in its forecourt beneath the stag; John Charles Maggs’s coaching scene of the inn (demolished 1867).
image.credit: Victoria Art Gallery, Bath (via Art UK) · John Charles Maggs · BATVG P 1984.41 · public domain

pins.name: the gravel walk
pins.story: The quiet gravelled walk, apart from the crowd at last, where Anne and Wentworth say everything eight years had left unsaid, and settle the second chance the world had all but taken from them. The happiest, most sensible walk in Bath.

image.caption: The Royal Crescent, Bath; the gravel walk runs along the foot of its lawn, where Anne and Wentworth are reconciled. Thomas Malton’s 1780 aquatint.
image.credit: Thomas Malton the Younger, 1780 · via Wikimedia Commons · public domain

pins.name: Westgate Buildings
pins.story: The poor lodging, low by the baths, of Anne’s old schoolfriend Mrs Smith - widowed, ill and penniless. Sir Walter is appalled that Anne would visit so mean an address; but it is here Anne learns the truth about Mr Elliot, a cold fortune-hunter behind the charm.
